KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Conceptual) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model] **Differentiate between the three levels of a product. Give an example for each level using a car.** **Model Answer:** The three levels of a product (Kotler) are: - **Core Product:** The fundamental benefit or value the customer seeks. For a car, the core product is *convenient transportation, mobility, and personal freedom*. - **Actual Product:** The physical, tangible attributes — features, design, quality, brand name, and packaging. For a car, the actual product is the *Hyundai Creta with 1.5L diesel engine, white colour, sunroof, touchscreen, Creta branding*. - **Augmented Product:** Additional, non-physical value beyond the customer's expectations. For a car, this is the *3-year warranty, free roadside assistance, free first service, complimentary insurance, loan facility, and 24×7 customer helpline*. *(3 marks: 1 mark per level with example)* ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model] **Explain the concept of an "Unsought Product" with two suitable examples.** **Model Answer:** An unsought product is one for which the **consumer does not have prior awareness or active desire** to purchase. It requires **aggressive selling and advertising** because the customer will not actively search for it. **Examples:** 1. **Life Insurance Policy** — Most people do not proactively think of buying insurance until it is marketed heavily or triggered by a life event. 2. **Fire Extinguisher** — A household rarely buys it unless legally required or persuasively sold. Other valid examples: encyclopaedias, donations to charities, blood donation camps, gravestones. *(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 examples with 1 mark each)* --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice) ### Question A — 14 Marks [Recommended Practice Question] **Q3. (a)** Explain the **Product Life Cycle (PLC)** in detail. Describe the **four stages** of PLC with reference to **sales, profit, customer type, and marketing strategy**. (7 marks) **Q3. (b)** Classify **consumer products** into four categories. For each category, explain the **buying behaviour, price level, and distribution intensity** with one real-world example. (7 marks) --- #### Model Answer — Q3(a): Product Life Cycle (7 Marks) The Product Life Cycle (PLC) is a model that describes the **trajectory of a product's sales and profitability from launch to withdrawal** over its lifetime. It consists of **four sequential stages**, though the duration of each stage varies by industry. **Stage 1: Introduction** - Sales: very low; product is being launched. - Profit: **negative** due to high R&D, marketing, and distribution costs. - Customers: **Innovators** (2.5% of the market) — risk-takers. - Competitors: very few. - Marketing Strategy: *Skimming pricing* (high price, premium positioning) or *Penetration pricing* (low price, mass market). Heavy advertising to build awareness. - Example: *Tesla Cybertruck at launch* — high price, limited customers, heavy promotional spend. **Stage 2: Growth** - Sales: **rising rapidly** as product gains acceptance. - Profit: **rising** as economies of scale kick in. - Customers: **Early Adopters** (13.5%) — opinion leaders. - Competitors: **increasing rapidly** as the market becomes attractive. - Marketing Strategy: *Maximise market share*; improve product features; expand distribution; competitive pricing. - Example: *Apple iPhone in 2008–2010* — sales surged as competitors scrambled to enter. **Stage 3: Maturity** - Sales: **plateau at peak**; growth slows. - Profit: **stable or declining** due to intense competition. - Customers: **Early Majority (34%) and Late Majority (34%)** — pragmatists. - Competitors: many, with **stable market shares**. - Marketing Strategy: *Defend market share*; product differentiation; find new uses; packaging redesign; explore new geographic markets. - Example: *Colgate toothpaste, Maggi noodles, Samsung Galaxy S series* — all in continuous maturity with reformulations. **Stage 4: Decline** - Sales: **falling** due to market saturation, technology shift, or changing preferences. - Profit: **declining** as costs remain fixed. - Customers: **Laggards (16%)** — traditionalists. - Competitors: gradually exiting. - Marketing Strategy: **Harvest** (reduce marketing spend, milk the product), **Divest** (sell to another firm), or **Discontinue**. - Example: *BlackBerry phones, Nokia 1100, VCRs, DVDs, IPL teams of older franchises in some seasons*. > *(Awarding 7 marks: 1.5 marks per stage covering sales + profit, 0.5 marks for customer type, 0.5 marks for strategy, 1 mark for examples.)* --- #### Model Answer — Q3(b): Consumer Product Classification (7 Marks) Consumer products are classified by **shopping behaviour** and **buying effort** into four categories: | Category | Buying Behaviour | Price Level | Distribution Intensity | Example | |----------|------------------|-------------|------------------------|---------| | **Convenience** | Habitual, low-involvement, minimum effort | Low | **Intensive** (maximum outlets — kirana stores, vending machines) | **Coca-Cola 750ml**, Parle-G biscuits, newspaper | | **Shopping** | Comparison-based, high-involvement, evaluate alternatives | Medium–High | **Selective** (specialty stores, branded showrooms) | **Samsung 55-inch Smart TV**, LG refrigerator, formal shirts | | **Specialty** | Brand-loyal, unique, will not accept substitute, extensive search | High | **Exclusive** (very few outlets, premium stores) | **Rolex watch**, Rolls-Royce Phantom, Louis Vuitton handbag | | **Unsought** | Consumer unaware, requires aggressive selling | Varies | **Direct or intensive** | **LIC Jeevan Anand policy**, fire extinguisher, blood donation | **Key Strategic Insight:** As we move from Convenience → Unsought, the **buying effort increases** while the **distribution intensity** generally narrows. A *shopping product* is sold through *selective distribution* (e.g., Croma, Reliance Digital), while a *convenience product* is sold in *every kirana shop and paan stall*. > *(Awarding 7 marks: 1.5 marks per category with example + 0.5 marks for strategic insight.)* --- ### Question B — 14 Marks [Alternative Choice] **Q4. (a)** Define **Product Mix**. Explain its **four dimensions** (Width, Length, Depth, Consistency) with reference to a real FMCG or electronics company. Compute the dimensions for a hypothetical example. (7 marks) **Q4. (b)** Explain the **BCG Matrix** (Boston Consulting Group Growth-Share Matrix) in detail. Describe the **four quadrants** (Stars, Cash Cows, Question Marks, Dogs) with examples and the recommended strategy for each. (7 marks) --- #### Model Answer — Q4(a): Product Mix and its Four Dimensions (7 Marks) **Definition:** A **Product Mix** (also called Product Assortment or Product Portfolio) is the **complete set of all products and product lines** that a company offers for sale to its customers. **Example Reference: Hindustan Unilever Limited (HUL)** HUL's product mix includes product lines such as: - Personal Wash: *Lux, Dove, Clinic Plus, Hamam, Liril, Lifebuoy* - Hair Care: *Sunsilk, Clinic Plus, TRESemmé* - Skin Care: *Pond's, Fair & Lovely (now Glow & Lovely)* - Oral Care: *Close-Up, Pepsodent* - Fabric Care: *Surf Excel, Rin, Wheel* **The Four Dimensions:** 1. **Width (Breadth):** The number of *different product lines* in the mix. For HUL, Width ≈ 12+ product lines. 2. **Length:** The total *number of product items* across all lines. For HUL, Length ≈ 50+ items. 3. **Depth:** The number of *variations (sizes, flavours, formulations)* per item. For *Dove soap*, depth = 100g, 125g, 150g, 200g, 300g, 500g. 4. **Consistency:** The *closeness* of product lines in terms of use, distribution, production. HUL has **high consistency** because all are FMCG, all sold through grocery stores, all aimed at mass households. **Hypothetical Computation Example (already worked in Section 3.1.2):** $$ W = 3, \quad L_{mix} = 12, \quad D \approx 3.08, \quad \bar{L} = 4 $$ > *(Awarding 7 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1.5 marks for dimensions with HUL example, 1.5 marks for numerical example, 1 mark for consistency explanation, 1 mark for business interpretation, 1 mark for overall presentation.)* --- #### Model Answer — Q4(b): BCG Matrix (7 Marks) The **Boston Consulting Group (BCG) Growth-Share Matrix** is a portfolio planning tool developed by Bruce Henderson in 1970. It classifies a company's product lines or Strategic Business Units (SBUs) into **four quadrants** based on two dimensions: - **Market Growth Rate** (industry attractiveness) - **Relative Market Share** (competitive strength) **The Four Quadrants:** **1. STARS** *(Top-Left: High Growth + High Share)* - High market share in a fast-growing market. - Heavy cash investment needed to maintain leadership. - Often become Cash Cows in the next stage. - **Strategy: BUILD and INVEST.** Defend market position aggressively. - *Example:* Apple's iPhone (2010–2015), Tesla Model 3 (recent), Google Search in the 2010s. **2. CASH COWS** *(Top-Right: Low Growth + High Share)* - High market share in a slow-growth market. - Generate **excess cash** that can be used to fund Stars and Question Marks. - **Strategy: HOLD and HARVEST.** Minimal new investment; maximum cash extraction. - *Example:* Microsoft Office, Microsoft Windows (legacy), Lux soap, Parle-G, Coca-Cola (in mature markets). **3. QUESTION MARKS** *(Bottom-Left: High Growth + Low Share)* - Low market share in a high-growth market — a risky position. - Requires **heavy cash investment** to convert into Stars, or **divestment** to cut losses. - **Strategy: SELECTIVELY INVEST** in promising ones; divest the rest. - *Example:* Nokia smartphones (early 2010s — high-growth market, but losing share), Ola Electric (in EV market). **4. DOGS** *(Bottom-Right: Low Growth + Low Share)* - Low market share in a low-growth market — the worst position. - Generate just enough cash to maintain themselves. - **Strategy: DIVEST or LIQUIDATE.** Do not invest further. - *Example:* BlackBerry phones (after 2015), VCRs, pagers, Orkut (social network), CDs/DVDs. **Strategic Use of the Matrix:** - Companies should aim to have a **balanced portfolio** of Stars, Cash Cows, and selectively chosen Question Marks. - Cash from Cash Cows funds the growth of Stars and Question Marks. - Too many Dogs indicate strategic failure; too many Question Marks indicate a cash crunch. > *(Awarding 7 marks: 1 mark for introduction, 1.5 marks per quadrant with strategy and example.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **The #1 mistake** students make is **listing all four PLC stages without linking them to a real product.** Always pair a stage with at least one *real-world example*. This adds 1–2 marks. > 2. **Do NOT confuse "Product Line Width" with "Product Mix Width."** Width refers to the **number of product lines**, not product items. > 3. **In the BCG Matrix**, always justify your quadrant placement with **both axes** (growth rate + market share). A one-axis argument is incomplete. > 4. **For Consumer Products**, the four categories are **mutually exclusive**. Do not classify a product in two categories simultaneously. > 5. **Kotler's 3-Level model** is the **default**; only mention the **5-level extended model** if the question asks specifically for "Kotler's 5 levels" or "extended product levels." > 6. **In numerical Product Mix questions**, show **all substitution steps**; partial computation loses marks. Always state the **unit** (items, variants, lines). --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:** - ✅ **Product** = anything offered to satisfy a need/want (AMA definition). It includes **goods + services + ideas**. - ✅ **Three Levels (Kotler):** Core → Actual → Augmented. Read inside-out, write outside-in. - ✅ **Goods vs. Services:** Tangibility, Storability, Ownership Transfer, Variability, Inseparability. - ✅ **Four Consumer Product Categories:** Convenience, Shopping, Specialty, Unsought (mnemonic: **C–S–S–U**, in increasing order of buying effort). - ✅ **Product Mix Dimensions:** Width (no. of lines), Length (total items), Depth (variants per item), Consistency (closeness). - ✅ **PLC Stages:** Introduction, Growth, Maturity, Decline. S-shaped sales curve. - ✅ **PLC Stage Identification:** Look for *sales trend, profit trend, customer type (innovator→laggard), and competitor count*. - ✅ **BCG Matrix Quadrants:** Stars (Build), Cash Cows (Hold), Question Marks (Selectively Invest), Dogs (Divest). - ✅ **Differentiation** is the act of *making* a product distinct; **Positioning** is the *place* the product occupies in the consumer's mind. - ✅ **Strategies for PLC stages:** Introduction = Skim/Penetrate; Growth = Maximise Share; Maturity = Defend/Differentiate; Decline = Harvest/Divest. - ✅ **Famous Indian Examples:** Maggi (Maturity with periodic revival), Royal Enfield (Niche Specialty), Lakme (Augmented-heavy brand), Tata Nano (Decline example). - ✅ **For 14-mark answers:** Always include a **table or diagram** — KTU examiners reward visual structuring. - ✅ **For 3-mark answers:** Definition + 2 supporting points is the golden structure. - ✅ **Avoid** mixing up "product line" with "product item." Line = group of items; Item = single SKU. - ✅ **Consistency** is *qualitative*, not quantitative — there is no formula for it in KTU exams. - ✅ **Marketing mix implication:** Each PLC stage demands a different **4P (Product, Price, Place, Promotion)** strategy.

topics.title: Product Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET753 - PRODUCT AND BRAND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Product Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Product Planning** is the strategic management process of defining, designing, developing, positioning, and managing a company's product offerings throughout their lifecycle to satisfy customer needs, achieve organizational objectives, and create sustainable competitive advantage in the marketplace.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme PEPET753 syllabus, Product Planning encompasses the systematic decision-making process that involves identifying market opportunities, conceptualizing products, defining product features, classifying offerings, determining the product mix, and planning product line extensions or deletions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Focus Area:** The syllabus specifically emphasizes the **strategic, tactical, and operational dimensions** of product planning — not just the act of "creating a product," but the holistic orchestration of portfolio decisions, lifecycle planning, and market alignment.

### Expanded Definition Components

A *product* in modern marketing is defined as **anything that can be offered to a market for attention, acquisition, use, or consumption that might satisfy a want or need**. It includes:

- Physical goods (tangible items like smartphones, cars)
- Services (intangible offerings like banking, healthcare)
- Ideas (concepts, causes, social messaging)
- Experiences (events, tourism, entertainment)
- Persons (celebrities, consultants)
- Places (tourist destinations, real estate)
- Organizations (NGOs, corporations)
- Information (databases, consultancy reports)

> [!NOTE]
> **Philip Kotler's Triadic Product Model** (frequently tested): A product has three layered dimensions — *Core Product* (the fundamental benefit), *Actual Product* (the features, design, brand, packaging), and *Augmented Product* (the additional services and benefits beyond expectations).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

**The "House Blueprint" Analogy:**

Imagine you want to build a house. You don't just start laying bricks randomly. You first:
- Decide *what kind* of house (1BHK, villa, apartment)
- Plan the *layout* (rooms, kitchen, bathrooms)
- Choose *materials* (cement quality, wood type)
- Determine *budget and timeline*
- Think about *who will live there* (family size, needs)
- Plan for *future extensions* (extra floor, garden)

**Product Planning is exactly this — but for a market offering.** It is the "architectural blueprint phase" before a product hits the shelves. Just as a poorly planned house leads to structural failures, a poorly planned product leads to market failure, wasted R&D budgets, and brand erosion.

> [!TIP]
> **Student Memory Hook:** Think **P.R.O.D.U.C.T. P.L.A.N.** 
> **P**ortfolio analysis → **R**esearch market → **O**bjectives setting → **D**esign concept → **U**nderstand lifecycle → **C**ategorize → **T**est & launch.
> **P**osition → **L**ine decisions → **A**ugment → **N**urture.

---

## 1.3 Product Levels — The Three-Tier Framework

| Level | Definition | Example (Smartphone) |
|-------|------------|----------------------|
| **Core Product** | The fundamental benefit or problem-solving value | Communication & connectivity |
| **Actual Product** | The physical features, design, brand, packaging | 6.5" display, 128GB, brand name |
| **Augmented Product** | Additional services beyond expectations | Free cloud storage, 24/7 support, warranty |

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Extension — Five Product Levels** (Kotler, Keller, Chernev): The contemporary KTU-relevant model adds **Potential Product** (future enhancements the product *could* have) and **Generic Product** (the basic version category). This is a frequent 3-mark question.

---

## 1.4 Why Product Planning Matters — KTU 2024 Relevance

In the highly competitive Kerala and global markets, companies like *Nescafe*, *iPhone*, and *Maruti Suzuki* invest heavily in product planning because:

1. **High Failure Rate**: ~**80%** of new products fail within the first year (Bain & Company research).
2. **Resource Optimization**: Prevents wastage of capital, time, and human resources.
3. **Customer-Centricity**: Ensures products align with evolving consumer needs.
4. **Competitive Positioning**: Differentiates offerings in saturated markets.
5. **Risk Mitigation**: Structured planning reduces market uncertainty.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Examiners often ask "Why is product planning important?" — your answer must reference the **risk reduction, customer satisfaction, and competitive advantage** triad.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

## 2.1 The Product Planning Process — Detailed Phases

Product Planning is not a single event but a **continuous, cyclical process**. Below is the academically accepted framework used in KTU 2024 scheme evaluation:

### Phase-Wise Breakdown

**Phase 1: Market Opportunity Identification**
- Conduct market research, competitor analysis, customer surveys
- Identify unmet needs, gaps, and emerging trends
- Tools: SWOT, PESTEL, Porter's Five Forces

**Phase 2: Idea Generation & Screening**
- Internal sources: R&D, employees, brainstorming
- External sources: customers, suppliers, competitors, trade shows
- Screening criteria: feasibility, profitability, strategic fit

**Phase 3: Concept Development & Business Analysis**
- Develop detailed product concepts
- Estimate costs, revenues, ROI, break-even
- Prepare preliminary marketing plan

**Phase 4: Product Design & Development**
- Engineering design, prototype creation
- Branding, packaging, labeling decisions
- Patent filings, IP protection

**Phase 5: Test Marketing & Commercialization**
- Pilot launch in limited markets
- Full-scale launch with marketing mix
- Distribution and promotional rollout

**Phase 6: Product Life Cycle Management**
- Monitor performance through PLC stages
- Plan for extensions, modifications, or deletion
- Continuous improvement based on feedback

---

## 2.2 Product Classification Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

Products can be classified along multiple dimensions. The KTU syllabus emphasizes the following:

### A. Based on Durability & Tangibility

| Type | Definition | Example |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Durable Goods** | Non-perishable, used repeatedly | Refrigerator, car, laptop |
| **Non-Durable Goods** | Consumed in one or few uses | Soap, food items, shampoo |
| **Services** | Intangible, inseparable, variable | Banking, consultancy, healthcare |

### B. Based on Consumer vs. Industrial Use

| Type | Target Market | Example |
|------|---------------|---------|
| **Consumer Products** | End users for personal use | Toothpaste, TV |
| **Industrial Products** | Businesses for further processing | Raw materials, machinery |
| **Convenience Products** | Bought frequently with minimum effort | Salt, newspaper |
| **Shopping Products** | Compared on quality, price, style | Furniture, clothing |
| **Specialty Products** | Unique characteristics, brand loyal | Rolex, luxury cars |
| **Unsought Products** | Not actively sought | Insurance, funeral services |

---

## 2.3 KTU Framework Cheat Sheet (No Pipe Conflicts)

**Product Mix Dimensions:**

$$
\text{Width} = \text{Number of different product lines}
$$

$$
\text{Length} = \text{Total number of products across all lines}
$$

$$
\text{Depth} = \text{Variants of each product in a line}
$$

$$
\text{Consistency} = \text{How closely related the product lines are}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Conflict Avoidance:** Note the use of `\vert` in place of `|` characters inside math expressions to maintain markdown table integrity.

**Example Illustration:**
A company has:
- 3 product lines (Beverages, Snacks, Dairy) → **Width = 3**
- 5 products in Beverages, 4 in Snacks, 6 in Dairy → **Length = 15**
- 3 sizes of each beverage → **Depth = 3**
- All are food items → **High Consistency**

---

## 2.4 Product Line Decisions (KTU Frequently Tested)

A **Product Line** is a group of closely related products that function in a similar manner, are sold to the same customer groups, marketed through the same outlets, or fall within given price ranges.

### Four Strategic Product Line Decisions:

1. **Line Stretching**: Extending the product line beyond its current range
   - *Downward Stretch*: Adding lower-priced items (e.g., Apple → iPhone SE)
   - *Upward Stretch*: Adding premium items (e.g., Maruti → Maruti Nexa)
   - *Two-way Stretch*: Both directions simultaneously

2. **Line Filling**: Adding more items within the existing price range to reduce competition gaps

3. **Line Modernization**: Updating existing product line with contemporary features (e.g., iPhone 14 → 15)

4. **Line Pruning**: Deleting weak products to focus on profitable items (product line contraction)

---

## 2.5 Product Life Cycle (PLC) — Strategic Planning Tool

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The PLC concept is a high-weightage topic** in KTU 2024 PEPET753. Examiners love combining PLC with product planning questions.

$$
\text{PLC Stages: Introduction} \rightarrow \text{Growth} \rightarrow \text{Maturity} \rightarrow \text{Decline}
$$

Each stage requires distinct product planning strategies:

| PLC Stage | Sales Pattern | Customer Type | Marketing Focus |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|-----------------|
| **Introduction** | Slow, low | Innovators (2.5%) | Awareness creation, high promotion |
| **Growth** | Rapid increase | Early adopters (13.5%) | Differentiation, distribution expansion |
| **Maturity** | Peak, plateau | Early & late majority (68%) | Product modification, defensive strategy |
| **Decline** | Falling | Laggards (16%) | Harvesting or deletion |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Management Fields

Product Planning principles are applied across:

- **Manufacturing Engineering**: Design for Manufacturing (DFM), modular product architecture
- **Industrial Engineering**: Production planning, capacity planning, lean manufacturing
- **IT/Tech Industry**: Agile product management, MVP (Minimum Viable Product) development
- **Automotive**: Platform sharing, variant management (Toyota's TNGA platform)
- **Consulting**: Portfolio strategy for conglomerates like Tata Group, Reliance
- **Kerala Context**: Coir, spices, ayurveda, and tourism product planning

> [!TIP]
> **Local Industry Reference (for Kerala students):** *Eastern Condiments* (Kerala-based spice brand) successfully planned its product line by introducing ready-to-cook mixes, instant pickles, and masala variants — a textbook case of **line filling + line stretching**.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: Product Mix Analysis

### **Problem Statement:**
*XYZ Electronics Pvt. Ltd.* operates in Kerala with the following product portfolio:

- **Product Line A — Smartphones**: 4 models, each available in 3 storage variants (64GB, 128GB, 256GB)
- **Product Line B — Laptops**: 5 models, each available in 2 RAM variants (8GB, 16GB)
- **Product Line C — Smart Watches**: 3 models, each available in 2 strap variants (Leather, Silicone)

**Calculate the Width, Length, Depth, and Consistency of the product mix.**

---

### **Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the Number of Product Lines**

The company operates in three distinct product categories:
- Smartphones
- Laptops
- Smart Watches

$$
\text{Width} = \text{Number of Product Lines} = 3
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Total Number of Products (Length)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Length} &= \sum (\text{Number of models in each line}) \\
&= (\text{Smartphones}) + (\text{Laptops}) + (\text{Smart Watches}) \\
&= 4 + 5 + 3 \\
&= 12
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Average Depth (Variants per Product)**

For Smartphones:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Depth}_{\text{Smartphones}} &= \text{Storage Variants} = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

For Laptops:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Depth}_{\text{Laptops}} &= \text{RAM Variants} = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

For Smart Watches:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Depth}_{\text{Smart Watches}} &= \text{Strap Variants} = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

Average Depth:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Average Depth} &= \frac{(4 \times 3) + (5 \times 2) + (3 \times 2)}{4 + 5 + 3} \\
&= \frac{12 + 10 + 6}{12} \\
&= \frac{28}{12} \\
&\approx 2.33
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Assess Consistency**

All three product lines fall under the **consumer electronics** category and are marketed through similar retail outlets (electronic showrooms, online platforms) to similar customer demographics. Hence:

$$
\text{Consistency} = \text{High}
$$

**Final Analytical Matrix:**

| Metric | Value | Interpretation |
|--------|-------|----------------|
| **Width** | 3 | Three distinct product lines |
| **Length** | 12 | Total number of individual products |
| **Average Depth** | ~2.33 | ~2-3 variants per model |
| **Consistency** | High | Related consumer electronics family |

---

## 3.2 Product Life Cycle Stage Identification — Analytical Method

### **Step-by-Step Diagnostic Framework:**

When classifying a product's PLC stage, analyze the following indicators:

**Step 1: Examine Sales Trend**

$$
\text{Growth Rate} = \frac{\text{Sales}_{\text{Current}} - \text{Sales}_{\text{Previous}}}{\text{Sales}_{\text{Previous}}} \times 100\%
$$

- **< 0%** → Likely **Decline**
- **0% to 10%** → Possibly **Maturity**
- **10% to 50%** → Likely **Growth**
- **Highly variable, low absolute value** → Possibly **Introduction**

**Step 2: Analyze Competitive Intensity**

$$
\text{Number of Competitors at each stage} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Few (1-3)} & \rightarrow \text{Introduction} \\
\text{Rising (3-7)} & \rightarrow \text{Growth} \\
\text{Many, intense (7+)} & \rightarrow \text{Maturity} \\
\text{Declining (back to 3-4)} & \rightarrow \text{Decline}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 3: Profit Pattern**

$$
\text{Profit Behavior} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Negative/Low} & \rightarrow \text{Introduction} \\
\text{Rising} & \rightarrow \text{Growth} \\
\text{Peak, then plateau} & \rightarrow \text{Maturity} \\
\text{Falling} & \rightarrow \text{Decline}
\end{cases}
$$

---

## 3.3 BCG Matrix — Portfolio Planning Tool (Detailed Application)

The **Boston Consulting Group (BCG) Matrix** is a strategic product planning tool that classifies products based on two dimensions:

$$
\text{Market Share} \times \text{Market Growth} \rightarrow \text{Strategic Category}
$$

### The Four Quadrants:

| Quadrant | Market Growth | Relative Market Share | Strategy |
|----------|---------------|------------------------|----------|
| **Stars** | High | High | Invest heavily, build dominance |
| **Cash Cows** | Low | High | Harvest cash flow, minimal investment |
| **Question Marks** | High | Low | Selective investment or divest |
| **Dogs** | Low | Low | Divest or liquidate |

### Worked Numerical Example:

Consider three products of a Kerala-based FMCG company:

- **Product P1**: Market Growth = 18%, Market Share = 25%
- **Product P2**: Market Growth = 5%, Market Share = 30%
- **Product P3**: Market Growth = 22%, Market Share = 4%

Assuming threshold values are: Market Growth ≥ 10% (high), Market Share ≥ 20% (high)

**Classification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{P1} &: \text{Growth} = 18\% > 10\%, \text{Share} = 25\% > 20\% \Rightarrow \textbf{STAR} \\
\text{P2} &: \text{Growth} = 5\% < 10\%, \text{Share} = 30\% > 20\% \Rightarrow \textbf{CASH COW} \\
\text{P3} &: \text{Growth} = 22\% > 10\%, \text{Share} = 4\% < 20\% \Rightarrow \textbf{QUESTION MARK}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Strategic Recommendations:**

- **P1 (Star)**: Invest aggressively to convert into a future Cash Cow
- **P2 (Cash Cow)**: Use generated cash to fund P3's development
- **P3 (Question Mark)**: Either invest heavily to convert into a Star, or divest

> [!NOTE]
> **Strategic Implication:** A well-planned product portfolio should ideally have a mix of Stars, Cash Cows, and selective Question Marks, while minimizing Dogs.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: The Product Planning Process (Complete Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["A: Opportunity Identification"] --> node2["B: Idea Generation"]
    node2 --> node3["C: Idea Screening"]
    node3 --> node4["D: Concept Development"]
    node4 --> node5["E: Business Analysis"]
    node5 --> node6["F: Product Design and Development"]
    node6 --> node7["G: Test Marketing"]
    node7 --> node8["H: Commercial Launch"]
    node8 --> node9["I: Life Cycle Management"]
    node9 --> node10["J: Extension or Deletion Decision"]
    node10 --> node1
    node9 -.Feedback Loop.-> node2
```

> [!TIP]
> **Read Direction:** Top-to-bottom represents chronological flow. The dotted feedback loop from Stage I back to Stage B represents the **continuous improvement principle** of product planning.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Product Levels Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph coreBlock["TIER 1: Core Layer"]
        coreA["CORE PRODUCT - Fundamental Benefit"]
    end
    subgraph actualBlock["TIER 2: Actual Layer"]
        actualA["ACTUAL PRODUCT - Features, Quality, Design, Brand, Packaging"]
    end
    subgraph augmentBlock["TIER 3: Augmented Layer"]
        augmentA["AUGMENTED PRODUCT - Warranty, Support, Delivery, Installation"]
    end
    subgraph potentialBlock["TIER 4: Potential Layer"]
        potentialA["POTENTIAL PRODUCT - Future Upgrades and Enhancements"]
    end
    coreA --> actualA
    actualA --> augmentA
    augmentA --> potentialA
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Architecture: Product Mix Decision Framework

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeInput["Company Product Portfolio"] --> nodeWidth["WIDTH Decision: Number of Product Lines"]
    nodeInput --> nodeLength["LENGTH Decision: Total Product Count"]
    nodeInput --> nodeDepth["DEPTH Decision: Variants per Product"]
    nodeInput --> nodeConsist["CONSISTENCY Decision: Inter-line Relatedness"]
    nodeWidth --> nodeStrategy["STRATEGIC PORTFOLIO DECISION"]
    nodeLength --> nodeStrategy
    nodeDepth --> nodeStrategy
    nodeConsist --> nodeStrategy
    nodeStrategy --> nodeOut1["Line Stretching"]
    nodeStrategy --> nodeOut2["Line Filling"]
    nodeStrategy --> nodeOut3["Line Modernization"]
    nodeStrategy --> nodeOut4["Line Pruning"]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Concept Map: Product Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeRoot["PRODUCT CLASSIFICATION"] --> nodeA1["Based on Tangibility"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeA2["Based on User Type"]
    nodeA1 --> nodeA1a["Durable Goods"]
    nodeA1 --> nodeA1b["Non-Durable Goods"]
    nodeA1 --> nodeA1c["Services"]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA2a["Consumer Products"]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA2b["Industrial Products"]
    nodeA2a --> nodeA2a1["Convenience"]
    nodeA2a --> nodeA2a2["Shopping"]
    nodeA2a --> nodeA2a3["Specialty"]
    nodeA2a --> nodeA2a4["Unsought"]
```

---

## 4.5 Mermaid BCG Matrix Quadrant Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph q1["HIGH MARKET GROWTH"]
        nodeQ1["STARS: High Share - Invest"]
        nodeQ2["QUESTION MARKS: Low Share - Decide"]
    end
    subgraph q2["LOW MARKET GROWTH"]
        nodeQ3["CASH COWS: High Share - Harvest"]
        nodeQ4["DOGS: Low Share - Divest"]
    end
    q1 --> q2
    q1 --> nodeQ1
    q1 --> nodeQ2
    q2 --> nodeQ3
    q2 --> nodeQ4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Convention:** The upper half of the matrix represents high market growth (expansion phase), while the lower half represents mature/saturated markets. Left and right halves represent low and high relative market share respectively.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Product Planning. List any four components of the product planning process.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Product Planning is the systematic process of making decisions about what products a company will offer, how they will be developed, positioned, and managed throughout their lifecycle to meet customer needs and organizational goals.

**Four Components of Product Planning Process:**

1. **Market Opportunity Identification** — Recognizing gaps in the market through research
2. **Idea Generation and Screening** — Brainstorming and filtering feasible concepts
3. **Product Development and Testing** — Designing, prototyping, and test-marketing
4. **Product Life Cycle Management** — Monitoring and managing products across Introduction, Growth, Maturity, and Decline stages

> [!TIP]
> **3-Mark Strategy:** Always provide the *definition* (1 mark) + *enumeration with brief explanation* (2 marks). Avoid lengthy paragraphs in Part A.

---

### **Question 2: Explain the three levels of a product with a suitable example.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

According to Philip Kotler, every product has three layered levels:

**1. Core Product (Mark: 1)** 
The fundamental benefit or problem-solving value the customer seeks. *Example: For a drill, the core benefit is making holes efficiently, not owning the drill itself.*

**2. Actual Product (Mark: 1)** 
The physical features, design, quality, brand name, and packaging. *Example: A Bosch drill with 500W motor, ergonomic design, branded packaging.*

**3. Augmented Product (Mark: 1)** 
Additional services and benefits beyond expectations. *Example: Free repair warranty, replacement guarantee, customer support hotline, free delivery.*

The modern model adds two more levels: **Generic Product** (basic category) and **Potential Product** (future enhancements).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Students often confuse "Product Mix" with "Product Line"** — these are different concepts. Product Mix is the *full set* of all products; Product Line is a *single category* within that mix.
> 2. **In PLC questions, students forget to mention marketing strategy changes** at each stage — losing 2-3 marks.
> 3. **BCG Matrix answers without threshold values** are penalized — always state your assumed thresholds for market growth (typically 10%) and market share (typically 20%).
> 4. **Avoid generic answers** like "good planning is important" — back every point with a *conceptual framework* or *example*.

---

### **Question A: Comprehensive Product Planning Analysis** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the major stages involved in the product planning process. Discuss the importance of test marketing in product planning.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The product planning process involves the following major stages:

**Stage 1: Opportunity Identification and Idea Generation (Mark: 1)**
The company identifies market gaps, customer needs, and emerging trends through market research, customer feedback, competitor analysis, and technological scanning. Ideas can come from internal sources (R&D, employees) or external sources (customers, suppliers, distributors).

**Stage 2: Idea Screening (Mark: 1)**
Concepts are filtered using criteria such as strategic fit, technical feasibility, market potential, and ROI estimation. Weak ideas are eliminated early to save costs.

**Stage 3: Concept Development and Business Analysis (Mark: 1.5)**
Detailed product concepts are crafted. Business analysis involves cost estimation, sales forecasting, break-even analysis, and profitability projections.

**Stage 4: Product Design and Development (Mark: 1)**
Engineering design, prototype creation, branding decisions, packaging design, and patent protection are undertaken.

**Stage 5: Test Marketing (Mark: 1.5)**
*(See detailed explanation below)*

**Stage 6: Commercialization (Mark: 0.5)**
Full-scale market launch with complete marketing mix deployment.

**Stage 7: Product Life Cycle Management (Mark: 0.5)**
Continuous monitoring, modification, and eventual deletion or extension decisions.

**Importance of Test Marketing in Product Planning:**

1. **Real-World Validation (Mark: 0.5)**: Test marketing provides authentic consumer feedback in real market conditions, unlike simulated lab tests.

2. **Risk Mitigation (Mark: 0.5)**: It reduces the risk of a full-scale launch failure by identifying potential issues with the product, packaging, pricing, or promotion.

3. **Sales Forecasting Accuracy (Mark: 0.5)**: Pilot markets provide data to refine sales projections and inventory planning for national rollout.

4. **Channel and Distribution Testing (Mark: 0.5)**: Companies can evaluate different retail channels, logistics arrangements, and merchandising approaches before full deployment.

5. **Refinement of Marketing Mix (Mark: 0.5)**: Test marketing allows tweaking of pricing strategies, promotional messages, and advertising channels based on actual customer response.

---

**(b) Discuss the concept of Product Life Cycle (PLC). Explain the characteristics and marketing strategies for the Maturity stage with suitable examples.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept of Product Life Cycle (Mark: 1)**

The Product Life Cycle is a strategic concept that describes the stages a product goes through from introduction to withdrawal from the market. It comprises four stages: **Introduction, Growth, Maturity, and Decline**. Each stage exhibits distinct sales, profit, customer, and competitive patterns, requiring different marketing strategies.

**Mathematical Representation:**

$$
\text{PLC: } S(t) = f(t), \text{ where } S(t) \text{ is sales at time } t
$$

The general shape resembles an S-curve followed by decline, though real-world patterns vary.

**Characteristics of the Maturity Stage (Mark: 2.5)**

1. **Sales Peak and Plateau**: Sales reach their highest level but growth slows. The product has achieved wide market acceptance.

2. **Intense Competition**: Many competitors have entered the market, leading to price wars and aggressive promotional activities.

3. **Market Saturation**: Most potential customers have already purchased the product; remaining prospects are hard to convert.

4. **Stable or Declining Profits**: Despite high sales, profit margins shrink due to competitive pricing pressure.

5. **Customer Base**: Primarily the *early majority* and *late majority* (approximately 68% of the market combined).

**Marketing Strategies for Maturity Stage (Mark: 2.5)**

1. **Market Modification**: Find new users or expand usage (e.g., Maggi noodles adding breakfast variants).

2. **Product Modification**: Modify features, quality, or style to attract repeat purchases (e.g., iPhone 14 → iPhone 15 upgrades).

3. **Marketing Mix Modification**: Adjust pricing, promotion, or distribution (e.g., discounts, loyalty programs).

4. **Brand Repositioning**: Target new market segments or geographies (e.g., exporting Kerala spices to international markets).

**Suitable Example (Mark: 1):**

*Colgate toothpaste in India* is in the maturity stage. Colgate-Palmolive employs strategies like launching new variants (Colgate Total, Colgate Visible White), introducing ayurvedic versions (Colgate Vedshakti), and heavy promotional campaigns during festive seasons to sustain market leadership.

---

### **Question B: Alternative Comprehensive Question** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Define Product Mix. Explain its four dimensions with a suitable example of an Indian company.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of Product Mix (Mark: 1)**

Product Mix (also called Product Assortment) refers to the complete set of all products and product lines that a company offers to the market. It is characterized by four key dimensions: Width, Length, Depth, and Consistency.

**The Four Dimensions of Product Mix (Mark: 4.5 — distributed as below)**

**1. Width (Mark: 1)**
Width refers to the *number of different product lines* a company carries.

*Example:* Hindustan Unilever Limited (HUL) has product lines in personal care, home care, foods, and beverages — width of approximately 4 major lines.

**2. Length (Mark: 1)**
Length is the *total number of products* across all product lines.

*Example:* If HUL has 50 individual products across its 4 lines, the length is 50.

**3. Depth (Mark: 1)**
Depth refers to the *number of variants* of each product (size, color, flavor, etc.).

*Example:* Dove shampoo may come in 4 sizes (90ml, 180ml, 400ml, 650ml) and 3 variants (dry hair, oily hair, normal hair) — depth of 12 variants.

**4. Consistency (Mark: 1.5)**
Consistency describes how closely related the various product lines are in terms of end-use, production requirements, distribution channels, or customer groups.

*Example:* HUL has high consistency because most of its products are fast-moving consumer goods (FMCG) sold through grocery retail channels and aimed at household consumers.

**Strategic Importance (Mark: 1.5)**

- Companies adjust product mix dimensions to grow sales, leverage brand equity, and balance risk
- **Width growth** diversifies risk; **depth growth** captures more market share within categories
- Example: ITC Limited expanded width from tobacco into FMCG, hotels, paperboards, and agribusiness — a textbook case of strategic width expansion.

---

**(b) Explain the BCG Matrix as a product planning tool. With a suitable example, classify four products of a company into the four quadrants.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**BCG Matrix Concept (Mark: 1.5)**

The Boston Consulting Group (BCG) Matrix, developed by Bruce Henderson in 1970, is a portfolio planning tool that classifies a company's products based on two dimensions:
1. **Market Growth Rate** (industry attractiveness)
2. **Relative Market Share** (competitive position)

Products are categorized into four quadrants: **Stars, Cash Cows, Question Marks, and Dogs**.

**Mathematical Foundation (Mark: 0.5)**

$$
\text{Relative Market Share} = \frac{\text{Company's Market Share}}{\text{Largest Competitor's Market Share}}
$$

A relative market share > 1.0 is considered "high." Market growth rate above the industry average (often 10%) is considered "high."

**The Four Quadrants (Mark: 3)**

| Quadrant | Characteristics | Strategy |
|----------|----------------|----------|
| **Stars** | High growth, high share | Invest heavily to build dominance |
| **Cash Cows** | Low growth, high share | Harvest cash, minimal investment |
| **Question Marks** | High growth, low share | Invest selectively or divest |
| **Dogs** | Low growth, low share | Harvest, divest, or liquidate |

**Worked Example — ITC Limited Portfolio (Mark: 2):**

Consider ITC's four product categories:

- **Product A — ITC Cigarettes**: Low growth (~3%), High market share (~75%) → **CASH COW**
- **Product B — ITC Sunfeast (Foods)**: High growth (~18%), High share among new entrants → **STAR**
- **Product C — ITC e-Choupal (Agri-tech)**: High growth (~20%), Low market share (~5%) → **QUESTION MARK**
- **Product D — ITC's older paperboard line**: Low growth (~4%), Low share → **DOG**

**Strategic Recommendations:**

- Cash Cows (Cigarettes) generate cash to fund Stars (Sunfeast) and Question Marks (e-Choupal)
- Dogs are evaluated for pruning or harvesting
- The portfolio strategy ensures balanced resource allocation

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Revision Pointers — Master These Before the Exam:**

- ✅ **Product** = Anything offered to satisfy a need/want (Kotler definition)
- ✅ **Three Product Levels** = Core → Actual → Augmented (with Modern extension: Generic + Potential)
- ✅ **Product Planning** is a continuous, cyclical, strategic process — not a one-time activity
- ✅ **Four Major Stages** of Product Planning Process: Opportunity ID → Development → Test Marketing → Commercialization & PLC Management
- ✅ **Product Mix** has 4 dimensions: **Width, Length, Depth, Consistency** (memorize with the mnemonic **W-L-D-C**)
- ✅ **Product Line Decisions**: Stretching (upward/downward/two-way), Filling, Modernization, Pruning
- ✅ **PLC Stages**: **I-G-M-D** = Introduction → Growth → Maturity → Decline
- ✅ **PLC Strategy differs per stage**: Awareness (Intro) → Differentiation (Growth) → Modification (Maturity) → Harvest/Delete (Decline)
- ✅ **BCG Matrix** = 2×2 grid on Market Growth × Market Share → Stars, Cash Cows, Question Marks, Dogs
- ✅ **Test Marketing** is crucial for risk reduction, real-world validation, and marketing mix refinement
- ✅ **High Failure Rate**: ~80% of new products fail — emphasizes importance of structured planning
- ✅ **Consumer Products** = Convenience, Shopping, Specialty, Unsought (CSSU mnemonic)
- ✅ **Always link theory to real-world examples** in KTU answers (e.g., ITC, HUL, Maggi, Apple) for higher marks

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** In KTU 2024 Scheme PEPET753 exams, examiners reward *structured answers*, *diagrams (even hand-drawn)*, and *real-world examples*. Always allocate 2-3 minutes of your exam time to outlining your answer with headings before writing.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Product Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET753 - PRODUCT AND BRAND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Product Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Product Classification** is the systematic process of grouping products and services into distinct categories based on shared characteristics such as **durability, tangibility, consumer usage behavior, shopping habits, and end-use application**. Under the KTU 2024 Scheme for *Product and Brand Management*, classification forms the **foundational analytical framework** upon which subsequent marketing-mix decisions (the 4Ps), branding strategies, and product portfolio analysis are constructed.

According to **Philip Kotler's Marketing Management framework** (the universally adopted reference for KTU management syllabi), every market offering can be classified along two primary dimensions:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Board Standard)**
> A **Product** is anything that can be offered to a market to satisfy a want or need. It includes physical goods, services, experiences, events, persons, places, properties, organizations, information, and ideas. *Product Classification* is the categorical mapping of these offerings into strategic clusters to enable targeted marketing decisions.

### The Three Product Levels (Kotler's Hierarchy)

Every product, regardless of its physical form, exists on **three integrated levels**:

| Level | Definition | Practical Example (Coca-Cola) |
|---|---|---|
| **Core Product** | The fundamental benefit or problem-solution the customer seeks | Refreshment and quenching of thirst |
| **Actual Product** | The tangible features, design, brand name, quality, and packaging | A 750ml glass bottle of Coca-Cola with its iconic red label |
| **Augmented Product** | Additional services, warranties, delivery, and post-purchase support | Free home delivery, customer helpline, satisfaction guarantee |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Explanation for First-Time Learners**

Imagine you walk into a giant supermarket. The store has arranged thousands of items into aisles and shelves — *biscuits here, soaps there, electronics upstairs, fresh fruits near the entrance*. **Product Classification works exactly the same way** — it is the *aisle arrangement system* of the marketing world. By grouping products that share similar buying behavior, customer expectations, or physical characteristics, marketers can apply **the same winning strategy** to all products in a group, rather than reinventing tactics for every single item.

Think of it like a **hospital triage system**: doctors don't treat a common cold and a cardiac arrest with the same urgency or protocol. Similarly, marketers don't promote milk (a daily, low-involvement purchase) the same way they promote a luxury car (a high-involvement, rare purchase). **Classification is the triage system of marketing strategy**.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in Product Classification

- **Product Item**: A specific version of a product (e.g., iPhone 15 Pro Max, 256GB, Gold).
- **Product Line**: A group of closely related product items (e.g., all variants of iPhone 15).
- **Product Mix (Width)**: The total number of product lines a company offers (e.g., Apple offers iPhones, Macs, iPads, Watches, AirPods).
- **Product Line Depth**: The total number of items within a single product line.
- **Product Mix Length**: The total number of items the company carries across all lines.
- **Product Mix Consistency**: How closely related the various product lines are in end-use, production, or distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**
> Under the **2024 NEP-aligned Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** framework, students must be able to **distinguish, classify, and justify the categorization** of any given product into the appropriate strategic group. This directly maps to **Course Outcome CO1**: *Understand the fundamental concepts of product management and brand strategy*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 The Two Master Dimensions of Classification

Products can be classified along **two master dimensions**, and every product in the marketplace falls somewhere within this 2x2 matrix.

### Dimension 1: Durability & Tangibility

| Category | Definition | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Durable Goods** | Tangible products that survive many uses (3+ years) | Refrigerator, Washing Machine, Car |
| **Non-Durable Goods** | Tangible products consumed in one use or a few uses | Soap, Toothpaste, Biscuits, Soft Drinks |
| **Services** | Intangible, inseparable, variable, perishable offerings | Banking, Education, Healthcare, Salon |

### Dimension 2: Use-Based Classification (Consumers vs. Industry)

This is the **single most important classification** for KTU examination purposes.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Consumer Products (Kotler's Framework)

Consumer products are goods and services purchased by **end-consumers for personal, family, or household use**. They are subdivided into **four strategic groups** based on **buying behavior**:

### A. Convenience Products
- **Definition**: Products the customer buys **frequently, immediately, and with minimum comparison and buying effort**.
- **Shopping Pattern**: Habitual, low-involvement, routine.
- **Marketing Strategy**: Wide distribution (intensive), low price, mass advertising, prominent shelf placement.
- **Examples**: Milk, bread, newspapers, chewing gum, matchboxes, salt, sugar.

### B. Shopping Products
- **Definition**: Products the customer compares on **suitability, quality, price, and style** before purchasing.
- **Shopping Pattern**: Moderate-involvement, planned, comparative.
- **Marketing Strategy**: Selective distribution, brand differentiation, point-of-sale displays, salesforce assistance.
- **Examples**: Furniture, clothing, used cars, smartphones, appliances.

### C. Specialty Products
- **Definition**: Products with **unique characteristics or brand identification** for which a significant group of buyers is willing to make a special purchase effort.
- **Shopping Pattern**: High-involvement, brand-loyal, non-comparative.
- **Marketing Strategy**: Exclusive or selective distribution, premium pricing, targeted advertising, no price competition.
- **Examples**: Rolex watches, Rolls-Royce cars, designer clothing (Louis Vuitton), specific luxury smartphones.

### D. Unsought Products
- **Definition**: Products the consumer **does not know about or does not normally think of buying**.
- **Shopping Pattern**: Zero-involvement (until triggered).
- **Marketing Strategy**: Aggressive advertising, personal selling, direct response marketing.
- **Examples**: Life insurance, fire extinguishers, encyclopedias, cemetery plots, blood donation services.

---

## 2.3 Classification of Industrial / Business Products

Industrial products are purchased by **businesses, institutions, or organizations** for further processing, use in operations, or resale. They are classified into **three broad categories**:

### A. Materials and Parts
- **Raw Materials**: Farm products (wheat, cotton), natural products (coal, iron ore), marine products (fish).
- **Manufactured Materials and Parts**: Component materials (iron, cement), component parts (small motors, tires).

### B. Capital Items
- **Installations**: Major equipment (factories, heavy machinery, DG sets).
- **Accessory Equipment**: Portable factory equipment (tools, office desks, forklifts).
- **Operating Supplies**: Short-lived goods and repair items (lubricants, coal, paper, pencils).

### C. Supplies and Services
- **Maintenance and Repair Services**: Window cleaning, pest control.
- **Business Advisory Services**: Legal, management consulting, advertising agency services.

---

## 2.4 Product Mix Decision Framework

A company's complete set of products is analyzed using **four dimensions**:

| Dimension | Definition | Formula / Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Width** | Number of distinct product lines | $\text{Width} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i$ |
| **Length** | Total number of items in the mix | $\text{Length} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} I_j$ |
| **Depth** | Number of variants per product line | $\text{Depth} = \frac{\text{Length}}{\text{Width}}$ |
| **Consistency** | Closeness of product lines in end-use | Qualitative Index (0–1 scale) |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Business Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Product Classification Matters in the Real World**

1. **Inventory Management**: Correct classification determines storage, handling, and shelf-life protocols.
2. **Marketing Budget Allocation**: Companies allocate spend based on convenience (high volume, low margin) vs. specialty (low volume, high margin) categories.
3. **Channel Strategy**: Intensive distribution for convenience goods; selective/exclusive for specialty.
4. **Pricing Strategy**: Cost-plus for raw materials; value-based for specialty; competitive for shopping.
5. **Brand Architecture**: Classification informs whether to use a **Master Brand**, **Sub-Brand**, or **Endorsed Brand** strategy.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Decision Framework

## 3.1 The Consumer Product Classification Decision Tree (Analytical Derivation)

To classify any given consumer product rigorously, a KTU student must walk through the following **decision logic chain**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{STEP 1: Identify the Buyer Type} \\
&\quad \text{Is the buyer an individual consumer or a business?} \\
&\quad \text{IF Consumer} \rightarrow \text{Go to STEP 2} \\
&\quad \text{IF Industrial} \rightarrow \text{Use Industrial Classification (Section 2.3)} \\
\\
&\textbf{STEP 2: Assess the Buying Effort (B)} \\
&\quad B = f(\text{Frequency}, \text{Involvement}, \text{Comparison}) \\
&\quad \text{Where } f \text{ is the cumulative cognitive and physical effort invested.} \\
\\
&\textbf{STEP 3: Apply Threshold Logic} \\
&\quad \text{IF } B = \text{Low} \rightarrow \textbf{Convenience Product} \\
&\quad \text{IF } B = \text{Moderate} \rightarrow \textbf{Shopping Product} \\
&\quad \text{IF } B = \text{High} \rightarrow \textbf{Specialty Product} \\
&\quad \text{IF } B = \text{None until triggered} \rightarrow \textbf{Unsought Product} \\
\\
&\textbf{STEP 4: Validate by Tangibility} \\
&\quad \text{Durable} \rightarrow \text{Tangible, multi-use} \\
&\quad \text{Non-Durable} \rightarrow \text{Tangible, single/limited use} \\
&\quad \text{Service} \rightarrow \text{Intangible, inseparable}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Product Mix Width & Depth — Numerical Derivation

> **Worked Example for KTU Board Exam**

**Problem:** A company named *Zenith Electronics* manufactures the following product items:
- **Product Line A (Smartphones)**: Zenith Air (4 variants), Zenith Pro (3 variants)
- **Product Line B (Laptops)**: Zenith Lite (2 variants), Zenith Ultra (3 variants), Zenith Creator (1 variant)
- **Product Line C (Smartwatches)**: Zenith Pulse (2 variants)

**Required:** Calculate (a) Product Mix Width, (b) Product Mix Length, (c) Average Product Line Depth.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{(a) Product Mix Width} \\
&\text{Width} = \text{Number of Product Lines} \\
&\text{Width} = 3 \quad [\text{Smartphones, Laptops, Smartwatches}] \\
\\
&\textbf{(b) Product Mix Length} \\
&\text{Length} = \sum \text{of all items across all lines} \\
&\text{Smartphones} = 4 + 3 = 7 \\
&\text{Laptops} = 2 + 3 + 1 = 6 \\
&\text{Smartwatches} = 2 \\
&\text{Length} = 7 + 6 + 2 = 15 \\
\\
&\textbf{(c) Average Product Line Depth} \\
&\text{Depth} = \frac{\text{Length}}{\text{Width}} = \frac{15}{3} = 5 \text{ items per line} \\
\\
&\textbf{(d) Consistency Index (Qualitative)} \\
&\text{All three lines are consumer electronics} \rightarrow \text{High Consistency}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Classification Matrix for Quick Reference

| Product | Tangibility | Durability | Buyer | Strategic Category |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Toothpaste | Tangible | Non-Durable | Consumer | Convenience |
| iPhone 15 Pro | Tangible | Durable | Consumer | Specialty / Shopping |
| Insurance Policy | Intangible | Service | Consumer | Unsought |
| Industrial Steel Coil | Tangible | Durable | Industrial | Raw Material |
| Company Delivery Truck | Tangible | Durable | Industrial | Capital Equipment |
| Office Cleaning | Intangible | Service | Industrial | Maintenance Service |
| Restaurant Meal | Tangible+Intangible | Non-Durable | Consumer | Service / Convenience |
| Designer Saree | Tangible | Durable | Consumer | Shopping / Specialty |

---

## 3.4 BCG Matrix — Strategic Product Portfolio Classification (Advanced)

The **Boston Consulting Group (BCG) Matrix** classifies a company's products into four quadrants based on **market growth rate** and **relative market share**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Quadrant} = f(g, s) \\
&\text{where } g = \text{Market Growth Rate (\%)} \\
&\text{and } s = \text{Relative Market Share}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Quadrant | Growth | Market Share | Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stars** | High | High | Invest aggressively for future dominance |
| **Cash Cows** | Low | High | Harvest cash flow, minimal new investment |
| **Question Marks** | High | Low | Selective investment or divestment |
| **Dogs** | Low | Low | Liquidate, reposition, or divest |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
