```mermaid
    PM[Product Mix] --> W[Width Number of Lines]
    PM --> L[Length Total Items]
    PM --> D[Depth Variants per Item]
    PM --> C[Consistency Closeness of Lines]
    W --> W1[Strategic Diversity]
    L --> L1[Total SKU Count]
    D --> D1[Customer Choice Breadth]
    C --> C1[Portfolio Cohesion Index]
    style PM fill:#312e81,color:#ffffff
    style W fill:#6366f1,color:#ffffff
    style L fill:#8b5cf6,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#a855f7,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#d946ef,color:#ffffff
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
> **Define Product Mix. Explain the four dimensions of product mix with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**
The product mix (also called product assortment) is the complete set of products and items that a particular seller offers for sale to customers. The four dimensions are:

1. **Width (Breadth):** The number of different product lines the company offers. *Example:* ITC has product lines in cigarettes, FMCG, hotels, paperboards — width = 4.

2. **Length:** The total number of products (items) within the entire product mix. *Example:* If ITC has 3 cigarette brands, 5 FMCG brands, 2 paperboard categories, the length = 10.

3. **Depth:** The number of variations of each product (size, color, flavor, formulation). *Example:* Aashirvaad Atta comes in 1kg, 5kg, 10kg — high depth.

4. **Consistency:** How closely related the product lines are in end-use, production, distribution, or target customers. *Example:* Nokia's earlier consistency was high (all mobile phones); today's mix is less consistent (mobiles + networks + Here maps).

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
> **Differentiate between Product Line Stretching and Product Line Filling. Mention the types of line stretching.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Product Line Stretching | Product Line Filling |
|---|---|---|
| **Meaning** | Extending the product line **beyond** its current price range | Adding more products **within** the existing price range |
| **Direction** | Upward, Downward, or Two-way | Same price-quality tier |
| **Objective** | Reach new customer segments or build prestige | Capture more shelf space, deter competitors, exploit excess capacity |
| **Example** | Maruti launching premium cars (Kizashi) | Maruti adding more variants to Swift (Lxi, Vxi, Zxi, Zxi+) |

**Types of Line Stretching:**
1. **Downward Stretch:** Premium brand launches a lower-priced variant (e.g., Apple $\to$ iPhone SE).
2. **Upward Stretch:** Economy brand launches a premium variant (e.g., Xiaomi $\to$ Mi Premium).
3. **Two-Way Stretch:** Brand expands in both directions (e.g., Samsung Galaxy series).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE:** Students must attempt **ONE** of the two questions (A or B). Each has sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Product Mix & Product Line Analysis

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
> **"Astro Mobile Solutions"** offers four product lines with the following details:
>
> | Product Line | Number of Products | Variants per Product |
> |---|---|---|
> | Smartphones | 5 | 4 |
> | Tablets | 3 | 3 |
> | Smartwatches | 2 | 5 |
> | Accessories | 4 | 2 |
>
> **(a)** Calculate the Product Mix Width, Length, and Average Line Depth. Also compute the total number of variants in the entire product mix. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** The company plans to introduce a new premium line of "Foldable Phones." Identify and explain which type of product line stretching strategy this represents. Also, suggest three risks the company must consider before launching this line. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Quantitative Analysis (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Product Mix Width:**
$$
\text{Width} = 4 \text{ (Smartphones, Tablets, Smartwatches, Accessories)}
$$

**Step 2 — Product Mix Length:**
$$
\text{Length} = 5 + 3 + 2 + 4 = 14 \text{ products}
$$

**Step 3 — Average Line Depth:**
$$
\text{Average Line Depth} = \frac{\text{Total Length}}{\text{Total Width}} = \frac{14}{4} = 3.5
$$

**Step 4 — Total Variants:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Smartphones} &= 5 \times 4 = 20 \\
\text{Tablets} &= 3 \times 3 = 9 \\
\text{Smartwatches} &= 2 \times 5 = 10 \\
\text{Accessories} &= 4 \times 2 = 8 \\
\text{Total Variants} &= 20 + 9 + 10 + 8 = 47 \text{ SKUs}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:** Width = 4; Length = 14; Average Line Depth = 3.5; Total Variants = 47.

**(b) Strategic Interpretation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the Stretching Type:**
The introduction of a "Foldable Phones" line at a premium price point (typically above the existing smartphone range) by an established mid-range player represents an **UPWARD STRETCH** along the price-quality axis.

**Step 2 — Justification:**
- The new line is positioned at a **higher price** than the existing smartphone offerings.
- It targets a **premium customer segment** (affluent professionals, tech enthusiasts).
- It is a **brand prestige play** — attempting to elevate the overall brand perception.

**Step 3 — Three Strategic Risks to Consider:**

1. **Cannibalization Risk:** Premium foldable phones may cannibalize the high-end sales of the existing smartphone line rather than attracting new customers.

2. **Brand Dilution Risk:** If the foldable phones fail due to quality issues, the entire brand reputation suffers — the parent brand becomes associated with premium-product failure.

3. **Resource Strain:** R\&D, marketing, and after-sales service for foldable phones require specialized investment, potentially stretching the company's operational and financial resources.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Product Life Cycle & BCG Matrix

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
> **"FreshBite Foods"** launched a new range of plant-based protein snacks called "GreenBite" two years ago. The product had first-year sales of ₹80 crores and second-year sales of ₹200 crores. The total industry size this year is ₹1,000 crores. The largest competitor in the plant-based segment, "VegPro," has sales of ₹250 crores. The industry's high-growth threshold is 15%.
>
> **(a)** Calculate the Market Growth Rate of GreenBite, its Market Share, and Relative Market Share. Identify the position of GreenBite in the BCG Matrix and state the strategic implications. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Explain the four stages of the Product Life Cycle (PLC) with suitable marketing strategies for each stage. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) BCG Matrix Analysis (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Market Growth Rate (MGR) of GreenBite:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MGR} &= \left(\frac{\text{Current Sales} - \text{Previous Sales}}{\text{Previous Sales}}\right) \times 100 \\
&= \left(\frac{200 - 80}{80}\right) \times 100 = \left(\frac{120}{80}\right) \times 100 = 150\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Market Share of GreenBite:**
$$
\text{Market Share} = \left(\frac{200}{1000}\right) \times 100 = 20\%
$$

**Step 3 — Relative Market Share (RMS):**
$$
\text{RMS} = \frac{\text{GreenBite Sales}}{\text{VegPro Sales}} = \frac{200}{250} = 0.8
$$

**Step 4 — BCG Quadrant Identification:**

- MGR = 150% (>> 15% threshold) → **High Growth** market
- RMS = 0.8 (< 1.0) → **Low Relative Share**

> **Quadrant: QUESTION MARK** (High Growth + Low Share)

**Step 5 — Strategic Implications:**

1. **Heavy Investment Required:** GreenBite is in a high-growth market but trails the leader; it must invest aggressively in marketing, distribution, and product innovation to convert into a Star.

2. **Selective Divestment Risk:** If the company cannot build competitive advantages (taste, price, distribution), it should consider harvesting or divesting.

3. **Cash Flow Concern:** Question Marks typically consume cash (negative cash flow) because the company is investing in future market position rather than harvesting current returns.

---

**(b) Product Life Cycle Stages & Strategies (7 marks):**

| Stage | Characteristics | Marketing Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Introduction** | Low sales, high costs, few competitors, awareness-building phase | **Pricing:** Skimming or Penetration. **Promotion:** Heavy awareness ads. **Distribution:** Selective. **Product:** Basic version only. |
| **2. Growth** | Rapid sales rise, profits peak, new competitors enter, product acceptance widens | **Pricing:** Slight reductions. **Promotion:** Differentiate from competitors. **Distribution:** Intensive expansion. **Product:** Add features and models. |
| **3. Maturity** | Sales plateau, intense competition, profit margins shrink, market saturation | **Pricing:** Competitive parity. **Promotion:** Reminder and loyalty ads. **Distribution:** Maximum intensity. **Product:** Line extensions, modifications. |
| **4. Decline** | Sales fall, profits drop, customer migration to newer substitutes | **Pricing:** Discounted clearance. **Promotion:** Minimal. **Distribution:** Selective pruning. **Product:** Phase out weak items, harvest or divest. |

**Conclusion:** Every product eventually moves through these stages. The marketer's job is to **delay decline**, **extend maturity**, and **plan new products** in advance to replace declining ones — ensuring the firm's portfolio remains healthy.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Product Mix Width" with "Length":** Width = number of LINES, Length = total number of PRODUCTS. Students frequently interchange these and lose 2-3 marks instantly.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Multiplier Effect in Depth:** When calculating total variants, students forget to multiply (products × variants). Remember: depth applies to each product individually.
>
> 3. **BCG Threshold Confusion:** The "Market Growth Rate" threshold of 10% or 15% is industry-specific. Always state the threshold before classifying the quadrant.
>
> 4. **One-Word Answers in PLC Questions:** Writing only "Introduction, Growth, Maturity, Decline" without explaining the **strategy for each stage** will fetch 0 marks. The strategy part carries 60% weightage.
>
> 5. **Skipping the "Why" in Stretching Questions:** Always justify WHY a strategy is upward/downward/two-way — examiners reward justification, not just labelling.
>
> 6. **No Diagram for PLC:** A simple S-curve sketch in your answer script earns 1-2 extra impression marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Product Strategy and Decisions**

- **Product Strategy** = the master plan for what the company offers, how it offers, and how it evolves.
- **Product Mix** has **4 dimensions:** Width, Length, Depth, Consistency.
- **Width = number of product lines;** Length = total products; Depth = variants per product.
- **Average Line Depth = Length ÷ Width.**
- **BCG Matrix** has 4 quadrants: **Stars, Question Marks, Cash Cows, Dogs.**
- **RMS > 1.0 = High Share;** RMS < 1.0 = Low Share.
- **High MGR + High RMS = STAR;** High MGR + Low RMS = QUESTION MARK.
- **PLC Stages:** Introduction $\to$ Growth $\to$ Maturity $\to$ Decline (4 stages).
- **Line Stretching:** Upward, Downward, Two-way.
- **Line Filling:** Adding products within existing range.
- **Line Pruning:** Deleting weak products to improve profit.
- **Line Modernization:** Updating design/technology of existing line.
- **NPD Process has 8 stages:** Idea Generation $\to$ Screening $\to$ Concept $\to$ Strategy $\to$ Business Analysis $\to$ Development $\to$ Test Marketing $\to$ Commercialization.
- **Product Differentiation** is achieved through features, quality, design, or service.
- **Product Positioning** = the place a product occupies in the consumer's mind vs. competitors.
- **Branding Decisions** include brand name selection, brand sponsorship, and brand development (line, range, umbrella, source).
- **Packaging** has 3 levels: Primary (consumer), Secondary (retailer), Transport (logistics).
- **Kerala Context Example:** Kerala Matta Rice, Tata Tea (Kanan Devan), Eastern Condiments — strong regional product strategies with differentiation via authenticity.
- **Quick Mnemonic:** "**W**ide **L**ines **D**eepen **C**onsistently" → **W-L-D-C** for Width, Length, Depth, Consistency.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Product Pricing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET753 - PRODUCT AND BRAND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Product Pricing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Product and Brand Management (PEPET753)**, **Product Pricing** is defined as the strategic marketing function of establishing a monetary value or exchange price at which a firm's goods or services are offered to its target market. According to **Philip Kotler's Marketing Management framework**, pricing is the only element of the marketing mix (4Ps) that generates revenue; all other elements (Product, Place, Promotion) represent costs.

Pricing is therefore not merely a numerical exercise but a **strategic positioning decision** that communicates a brand's value proposition, influences consumer perception, drives profitability, and directly impacts market share.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Product Pricing encompasses pricing objectives, factors influencing pricing decisions, pricing methods (cost-oriented, demand-oriented, competition-oriented), pricing strategies (penetration, skimming, premium, economy, psychological), and the administration of price changes including discounts, allowances, and price reactions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are at a **thriving flea market in Kerala**. Two vendors are selling the same handwoven banana-fibre bag:

- **Vendor A** prices it at ₹250, places it on a plain mat, and sells to bargain hunters.
- **Vendor B** prices it at ₹1,200, places it inside a glass case with elegant lighting, attaches an "artisan-crafted, handwoven by Kottayam artisans" tag, and plays soft music.

The **physical product is identical**, but Vendor B has created a *price-value perception*. The consumer perceives ₹1,200 as justified because the *branding, ambience, and storytelling* elevate perceived value. This is the essence of **Product Pricing** — price is not just a number; it is a **psychological and strategic weapon**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Terminology to Memorize for KTU:**
> - **List Price (MRP):** The official quoted retail price.
> - **Net Price:** The actual price paid after discounts.
> - **Markup:** The amount added to cost to arrive at selling price.
> - **Margin:** The percentage of selling price that is profit.
> - **Elasticity of Demand:** Sensitivity of quantity demanded to price changes.

### 1.3 The Strategic Significance of Pricing

Pricing is **P1 in the 4Ps** because it is:
1. The **fastest variable** to change (a price can be revised in hours; repositioning a product takes months).
2. The **only revenue-generating** marketing mix element.
3. A direct determinant of **profitability** and **cash flow**.
4. A **positioning signal** — premium pricing signals premium quality; economy pricing signals accessibility.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Pricing Process (6-Step Logical Framework)

The KTU syllabus emphasizes a structured approach to pricing decisions. The pricing process follows this logical sequence:

**Step 1 — Define Pricing Objective**
- Survival (during downturns)
- Maximum Current Profit
- Maximum Market Share (Penetration)
- Maximum Market Skimming (Premium)
- Product-Quality Leadership
- Status Quo (Competitive matching)

**Step 2 — Determine Demand Curve**
- Estimate the relationship between price and quantity demanded.
- Use **Price Elasticity of Demand (PED)** to measure responsiveness.

**Step 3 — Estimate Costs**
- Distinguish **Fixed Costs** (rent, salaries) from **Variable Costs** (raw materials, commissions).
- Compute the **Break-Even Point (BEP)**.

**Step 4 — Analyze Competitor Pricing**
- Benchmark prices against primary, secondary, and tertiary competitors.

**Step 5 — Select a Pricing Method**
- Cost-Oriented: Markup, Cost-Plus, Break-Even
- Demand-Oriented: Skimming, Penetration, Psychological
- Competition-Oriented: Going-Rate, Sealed-Bid, Discounting

**Step 6 — Set the Final Price**
- Apply psychological adjustments, discounts, allowances, and geographic considerations.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates every pricing formula a KTU 2024 student must know.

| **Formula Name** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Description / Variables** |
|---|---|---|
| Markup Pricing | $SP = Cost \times (1 + m)$ | $SP$ = Selling Price; $m$ = Markup % (as decimal) |
| Markup on Cost vs. Selling Price | $M_c = \dfrac{GM}{Cost}$ ; $M_s = \dfrac{GM}{SP}$ | $GM$ = Gross Margin (profit) |
| Price Elasticity of Demand | $E_p = \dfrac{\%\Delta Q}{\%\Delta P} = \dfrac{\Delta Q}{\Delta P} \times \dfrac{P}{Q}$ | $Q$ = Quantity demanded; $P$ = Price |
| Break-Even Point (Units) | $BEP_{units} = \dfrac{FC}{P - VC}$ | $FC$ = Fixed Cost; $VC$ = Variable Cost per unit |
| Break-Even Point (Revenue) | $BEP_{revenue} = \dfrac{FC}{1 - \dfrac{VC}{P}}$ | Alternative revenue-based form |
| Target Return Pricing | $P = VC + \dfrac{FC + TR}{Q}$ | $TR$ = Target Return (profit goal) |
| Profit Equation | $\pi = (P - AVC) \times Q - FC$ | $\pi$ = Profit; $AVC$ = Avg Variable Cost |
| Discount Calculation | $NP = LP \times (1 - d)$ | $NP$ = Net Price; $LP$ = List Price; $d$ = Discount rate |
| Psychological Pricing Adjustment | Odd Pricing $\approx P - \epsilon$ | Set slightly below round number, e.g., ₹999 instead of ₹1,000 |
| Profit Margin Conversion | $M_s = \dfrac{M_c}{1 + M_c}$ | Convert markup-on-cost to margin-on-selling |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering and Management Utility

- **Manufacturing Firms** use break-even and target-return pricing to determine launch prices for new SKUs (Stock Keeping Units).
- **E-commerce platforms** (Amazon, Flipkart) deploy dynamic, demand-based pricing algorithms based on elasticity.
- **SaaS and IT companies** use penetration pricing for user acquisition, followed by gradual price escalation.
- **Hospitality** uses yield management and psychological pricing (₹1,499/night vs. ₹1,500).
- **Engineering product companies** (e.g., Bosch, Tata Motors) use competition-oriented "going-rate" pricing in commodity segments.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU valuation:** Most Part B questions test whether you can **classify a strategy correctly**, **select the right formula**, and **show the computational steps**. Examiners reward structural answers that mirror the textbook framework.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Relationship Between Markup on Cost and Margin on Sales

In management accounting and pricing theory, the same gross margin can be expressed two ways: as a markup on **cost** ($M_c$) or as a margin on **selling price** ($M_s$). The conversion is a frequently asked derivation.

**Starting Premise:** Gross Margin equals Selling Price minus Cost.

$$GM = SP - Cost$$

**Step 1:** Markup on cost is gross margin divided by cost.

$$M_c = \frac{GM}{Cost} = \frac{SP - Cost}{Cost} = \frac{SP}{Cost} - 1$$

**Step 2:** Margin on selling price is gross margin divided by selling price.

$$M_s = \frac{GM}{SP} = \frac{SP - Cost}{SP} = 1 - \frac{Cost}{SP}$$

**Step 3:** From Step 1, isolate the ratio $SP/Cost$.

$$\frac{SP}{Cost} = 1 + M_c$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $SP/Cost$ into the Step 2 expression. Note that $Cost/SP = 1/(SP/Cost) = 1/(1+M_c)$.

$$M_s = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + M_c}$$

**Step 5:** Simplify by finding a common denominator.

$$M_s = \frac{(1 + M_c) - 1}{1 + M_c}$$

$$M_s = \frac{M_c}{1 + M_c}$$

**Final Derived Relationship:**

$$\boxed{M_s = \frac{M_c}{1 + M_c}}$$

**Inverse Conversion (Margin to Markup):**

$$M_c = \frac{M_s}{1 - M_s}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Break-Even Pricing

**Problem:** A KTU-startup produces reusable cotton bags. Fixed costs = ₹2,00,000 per month. Variable cost per bag = ₹40. The selling price is set at ₹120 per bag.

**Step 1 — Compute contribution per unit.**

$$\text{Contribution} = P - VC = 120 - 40 = \text{₹80 per bag}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Break-Even Point in units.**

$$BEP_{units} = \frac{FC}{P - VC} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{120 - 40} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{80} = 2{,}500 \text{ bags}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Break-Even Revenue.**

$$BEP_{revenue} = BEP_{units} \times P = 2{,}500 \times 120 = \text{₹3,00{,}000}$$

**Step 4 — Verify using revenue formula.**

$$BEP_{revenue} = \frac{FC}{1 - \frac{VC}{P}} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{1 - \frac{40}{120}} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{1 - 0.3333} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{0.6667} = \text{₹3{,}00{,}000}$$

The startup must sell **2,500 bags** to break even, generating **₹3,00,000** in revenue.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Price Elasticity & Decision Making

**Problem:** A local brand sells 5,000 units at ₹100. After raising price to ₹120, sales drop to 4,000 units. Determine elasticity and recommend a pricing action.

**Step 1 — Compute percentage change in quantity demanded.**

$$\%\Delta Q = \frac{4{,}000 - 5{,}000}{5{,}000} \times 100 = -20\%$$

**Step 2 — Compute percentage change in price.**

$$\%\Delta P = \frac{120 - 100}{100} \times 100 = +20\%$$

**Step 3 — Compute price elasticity of demand.**

$$E_p = \left|\frac{-20\%}{+20\%}\right| = 1.0$$

**Step 4 — Interpret.** Since $\vert E_p \vert = 1.0$ (unit elastic), revenue remains roughly constant. However, costs fall (fewer units produced) — so the firm retains the same revenue at lower cost. **Recommendation:** Marginal price increase is justified, but a further hike above 15% should be tested cautiously as elasticity may rise above 1.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Pricing Strategy Classification

| **Pricing Strategy** | **When Used** | **Typical Marketer** | **Customer Perception** | **Risk Profile** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Market Skimming | Innovative tech launches | Apple iPhones, Sony PS5 | Premium, exclusive | Cannibalization risk |
| Market Penetration | Mass-market entry, network effects | Jio, Xiaomi | Affordable, value-driven | Low margin pressure |
| Premium Pricing | Luxury, status signaling | Rolex, Gucci | High status, superior | Demand sensitivity |
| Economy Pricing | Cost-sensitive segments | D-Mart, Indigo (low-cost) | Practical, basic | Quality perception risk |
| Psychological Pricing | Retail, FMCG | All retail — ₹999 trick | Bargain illusion | Regulatory scrutiny |
| Dynamic Pricing | Perishable, demand-volatile | Uber, Ola, Airlines | Variable, fair-by-algorithm | Backlash if opaque |
| Bundle Pricing | Cross-sell, complementary goods | McDonald's meals, SaaS | Value perception | Cannibalization of singles |
| Captive Pricing | Razor-blade, ink-printer model | HP, Gillette | Initial low, recurring cost | Consumer resentment |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The 6-Step Pricing Decision Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Define Pricing Objective] --> B[Step 2: Estimate Demand Curve and Price Elasticity]
    B --> C[Step 3: Identify Fixed and Variable Costs]
    C --> D[Step 4: Analyze Competitor Pricing Structure]
    D --> E[Step 5: Select Pricing Method]
    E --> F[Step 6: Set Final Price with Psychological Adjustments]
    F --> G[Step 7: Monitor and Adjust Price over Time]
    G -. Feedback Loop .-> A
```

### 4.2 Taxonomy of Pricing Methods (Mermaid Mind-Style Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Root[Product Pricing Methods] --> CostOriented[Cost Oriented Methods]
    Root --> DemandOriented[Demand Oriented Methods]
    Root --> CompetitionOriented[Competition Oriented Methods]

    CostOriented --> CO1[Markup Pricing]
    CostOriented --> CO2[Cost Plus Pricing]
    CostOriented --> CO3[Break Even Pricing]
    CostOriented --> CO4[Target Return Pricing]

    DemandOriented --> DO1[Skimming Pricing]
    DemandOriented --> DO2[Penetration Pricing]
    DemandOriented --> DO3[Psychological Pricing]
    DemandOriented --> DO4[Dynamic Pricing]

    CompetitionOriented --> CP1[Going Rate Pricing]
    CompetitionOriented --> CP2[Sealed Bid Pricing]
    CompetitionOriented --> CP3[Discount and Allowance Pricing]
```

### 4.3 Pricing Strategy vs. Product Life Cycle Stage (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PLC[Product Life Cycle Stage] --> Intro[Introduction]
    PLC --> Growth[Growth]
    PLC --> Maturity[Maturity]
    PLC --> Decline[Decline]

    Intro --> S1[Skimming or Penetration Pricing]
    Growth --> S2[Competitive Parity Pricing]
    Maturity --> S3[Discount and Bundle Pricing]
    Decline --> S4[Harvesting or Economy Pricing]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams matter for KTU:** Drawing a clean block diagram in Part B answers can earn you 1–2 bonus marks for presentation. Examiners value structured visual answers, especially when describing pricing processes or taxonomies.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Define Product Pricing. List any three pricing objectives.

**Model Answer:**
Product pricing is the strategic marketing activity of determining the monetary value at which a product is offered to the target market. It is the only element of the marketing mix that generates revenue.

**Three pricing objectives:**
1. **Survival** — when the firm faces intense competition or recessionary demand.
2. **Maximum Current Profit** — setting price to maximize short-term profit margins.
3. **Market Share Leadership** — penetration pricing to capture the largest market share.

*(Any three of: Profit Maximization, Market Skimming, Sales Growth, Status Quo, Product Quality Leadership — accepted.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Distinguish between **Markup on Cost** and **Margin on Selling Price** with an example.

**Model Answer:**

- **Markup on Cost ($M_c$):** Profit expressed as a percentage of *cost*.  
  Example: A product costing ₹80 sold at ₹100 → $M_c = (100-80)/80 = 25\%$.

- **Margin on Selling Price ($M_s$):** Profit expressed as a percentage of *selling price*.  
  Same example: $M_s = (100-80)/100 = 20\%$.

**Key distinction:** Markup is computed on cost; margin is computed on selling price. They are mathematically related by $M_s = M_c / (1 + M_c)$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**a) (7 marks)** Explain the major **factors influencing the pricing decision** of a company with suitable examples.

**Model Answer:**

Pricing decisions are influenced by both **internal** and **external** factors:

**1. Internal Factors:**

- **Marketing Objectives:** If the firm aims for survival, it prices low; for premium positioning, it prices high.  
  *Example:* Xiaomi priced smartphones low during market entry (survival/share objective).
- **Marketing Mix Strategy:** A high-promotion, premium-quality product justifies premium pricing.  
  *Example:* Apple iPhone — premium pricing supported by premium promotion.
- **Costs:** Fixed and variable costs set a lower limit (floor price) for any pricing decision.  
  *Example:* Airlines must price above variable cost per seat to cover fuel and crew costs.
- **Organizational Considerations:** Pricing may be dictated by top management or be decentralized.

**2. External Factors:**

- **The Market and Demand:** Elasticity, buyer perception, and value perception influence pricing.  
  *Example:* Luxury goods have inelastic demand — buyers are price-insensitive.
- **Competitor Pricing:** Firms benchmark against competitors' prices.  
  *Example:* Telecom industry — Jio's entry forced rivals to drop tariffs.
- **Government and Legal Regulations:** Price ceilings, anti-profiteering laws.  
  *Example:* Drug price control in India via NPPA.
- **Economic Conditions:** Inflation, recession, interest rates.  
  *Example:* During COVID-19, premium product sales dropped; economy segment surged.

**Conclusion:** A holistic view of both internal and external factors ensures that pricing aligns with strategy, market reality, and regulatory compliance.

---

**b) (7 marks)** A company sells a product at ₹500 per unit. The variable cost is ₹300 per unit and total fixed costs are ₹4,00,000. Calculate:
   - (i) Break-Even Point in units and revenue.
   - (ii) Profit if 5,000 units are sold.
   - (iii) The new selling price required to achieve a target profit of ₹1,00,000 at 5,000 units.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $P = 500$, $VC = 300$, $FC = 4{,}00{,}000$.

**(i) Break-Even Point in units:**

$$BEP_{units} = \frac{FC}{P - VC} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{500 - 300} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{200} = 2{,}000 \text{ units}$$

**Break-Even Revenue:**

$$BEP_{revenue} = 2{,}000 \times 500 = \text{₹10{,}00{,}000}$$

**(ii) Profit at 5,000 units:**

$$\pi = (P - VC) \times Q - FC$$
$$\pi = (500 - 300) \times 5{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000$$
$$\pi = 200 \times 5{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000$$
$$\pi = 10{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹6{,}00{,}000}$$

**(iii) New Selling Price for ₹1,00,000 target profit at 5,000 units:**

$$P_{new} = VC + \frac{FC + \pi}{Q} = 300 + \frac{4{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000}{5{,}000}$$

$$P_{new} = 300 + \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{5{,}000} = 300 + 100 = \text{₹400 per unit}$$

**Result:** The new selling price must be **₹400 per unit** (₹100 reduction) to hit the ₹1,00,000 target at 5,000 units.

---

#### **Question B [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**a) (7 marks)** Differentiate between **Market Skimming** and **Market Penetration** pricing strategies. Under what conditions is each strategy most appropriate?

**Model Answer:**

| **Dimension** | **Market Skimming** | **Market Penetration** |
|---|---|---|
| **Price Level** | High initial price | Low initial price |
| **Objective** | Capture premium segment first | Capture mass market quickly |
| **Customer** | Innovators, early adopters | Mass-market, price-sensitive |
| **Time Horizon** | Short-term maximum profit | Long-term volume leadership |
| **Risk** | Cannibalization, competitor entry | Low margins, cash strain |
| **Examples** | Apple iPhone launch, Sony PS5 | Jio 4G launch, Xiaomi entry |

**When Market Skimming is appropriate:**
1. Product is genuinely innovative with no close substitutes.
2. Sufficient demand exists at a high price (inelastic demand).
3. High production capacity enables economies of scale later.
4. Premium image must be protected (luxury brands).

**When Market Penetration is appropriate:**
1. Market is highly price-sensitive (high elasticity).
2. Production cost falls sharply with scale.
3. Strong threat of competitors entering soon.
4. Goal is long-term market leadership.

**Conclusion:** Skimming maximizes early profit from eager adopters; Penetration builds volume and barriers to entry. The choice depends on demand elasticity, competitive intensity, and the firm's strategic objective.

---

**b) (7 marks)** A firm introduces a product with total cost ₹800 per unit. The firm wants a **20% markup on cost**. Compute:
   - (i) Selling price.
   - (ii) Margin on selling price.
   - (iii) New selling price if the firm instead targets a **20% margin on selling price**.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $Cost = 800$, $M_c = 20\% = 0.20$.

**(i) Selling Price with 20% markup on cost:**

$$SP = Cost \times (1 + M_c) = 800 \times (1 + 0.20) = 800 \times 1.20 = \text{₹960}$$

**(ii) Margin on Selling Price:**

$$GM = SP - Cost = 960 - 800 = 160$$

$$M_s = \frac{GM}{SP} = \frac{160}{960} = 0.1667 = 16.67\%$$

**(iii) New Selling Price for 20% margin on selling price:**

Using $M_c = \dfrac{M_s}{1 - M_s}$:

$$M_c = \frac{0.20}{1 - 0.20} = \frac{0.20}{0.80} = 0.25 = 25\%$$

$$SP_{new} = Cost \times (1 + M_c) = 800 \times 1.25 = \text{₹1{,}000}$$

**Verification:** $M_s = (1000-800)/1000 = 20\%$. ✓

**Result:** A **20% margin on selling price** requires a **₹1,000** selling price, compared to **₹960** for a 20% markup on cost.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Pricing Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing markup with margin.** Many students write markup as a percentage of selling price — losing 1–2 marks. Always clarify your denominator (cost vs. selling price) before computing.
> 2. **Forgetting units in BEP calculations.** Always write "BEP = 2,000 units" with the unit label. Examiners deduct marks for ambiguous answers.
> 3. **Skipping assumption statements.** In elasticity problems, state whether you are using the simple percentage method or the midpoint method.
> 4. **Failing to recommend.** In Part B case questions, merely computing is not enough — close with a managerial recommendation or implication. This often fetches the final 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Listing strategies without classification.** When asked to "explain pricing strategies," use headings and classifications (e.g., cost-oriented, demand-oriented) — bullet lists without structure cost marks.
> 6. **Ignoring the psychological dimension.** Pricing is not only mathematics; examiners expect at least one mention of psychological, brand-equity, or perception-related factors in 14-mark answers.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Product Pricing (Module 2):**

- **Definition:** Pricing = the marketing function of assigning monetary value to a product; the only revenue-generating P in the 4Ps.
- **Six-Step Process:** Objective → Demand Estimation → Cost Estimation → Competitor Analysis → Method Selection → Final Price Setting.
- **Five Pricing Objectives:** Survival, Maximum Profit, Market Share Leadership, Market Skimming, Quality Leadership, Status Quo.
- **Three Major Pricing Methods:**
  1. **Cost-Oriented:** Markup, Cost-Plus, Break-Even, Target-Return.
  2. **Demand-Oriented:** Skimming, Penetration, Psychological, Dynamic.
  3. **Competition-Oriented:** Going-Rate, Sealed-Bid, Discounting.
- **Critical Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $SP = Cost \times (1 + m)$
  - $E_p = (\Delta Q/\Delta P) \times (P/Q)$
  - $BEP_{units} = FC / (P - VC)$
  - $M_s = M_c / (1 + M_c)$
- **Strategy Selection Matrix:**
  - Innovation + Inelastic demand → **Skimming**
  - Mass market + Elastic demand → **Penetration**
  - Commodity + Competitive parity → **Going-Rate**
  - Status signaling + Status-conscious buyers → **Premium**
  - Recession + Cost-sensitive → **Economy**
- **Psychological Pricing Tools:** Reference pricing, odd/just-below pricing (₹999), price lining, bundle pricing.
- **Price Change Tactics:** Discounts, allowances, segmented pricing, promotional pricing, dynamic pricing.
- **Reactions to Price Changes:** Anticipate competitor response, customer switching behavior, supplier pressure, government regulation.
- **Key Exam Triggers:** Always classify before explaining; always state the formula; always show substitution steps; always conclude with managerial implication.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Product Packaging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET753 - PRODUCT AND BRAND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Product Packaging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Product Packaging (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> *Product Packaging is the coordinated system of preparing goods for transport, distribution, storage, retailing, consumption, and ultimate disposal, comprising all levels of containment — primary, secondary, and tertiary — integrated with labeling, branding, and informational elements that collectively influence consumer perception, utility, and purchase behavior at the point of sale.*

In the KTU Product and Brand Management framework, packaging is treated not merely as a protective wrapper but as the **"Fifth P" of the Marketing Mix** — alongside Product, Price, Place, and Promotion — and is recognized as a **silent salesman** and a tangible manifestation of the brand identity.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Conceptual Anchor:**
> Philip Kotler defines packaging as *"all the activities of designing and producing the container for a product."* The American Marketing Association (AMA) further extends this to include *"the container or wrapper plus its physical appearance and design, label, and brand name."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The Real-World Analogy

Think of packaging as the **"First Handshake"** between the product and the consumer. Just as a well-dressed, neatly groomed person at a job interview creates a strong first impression before speaking a word, the package is the very first physical touchpoint a customer encounters.

### Real-World Example: The Apple iPhone Box

Imagine you walk into a Croma showroom. Two smartphones lie on the counter — one in a sturdy, sleek white box with a magnetic latch and embossed logo, the other in a flimsy brown carton with a printed sticker. **Which one would you instinctively trust as premium?** This instinctive, visceral response is precisely what effective packaging engineering aims to engineer.

### A Second Analogy — The "Clothing" Metaphor

> [!TIP]
> Consider the human body. The product is the **soul/personality**, and the packaging is the **clothing + accessories**. Just as a corporate executive's attire signals competence, formality, and social standing, the package signals quality, value, category positioning, and brand prestige.

## 1.3 Three Fundamental Reasons Packaging Matters (The 3U Framework)

| Universal Truth | Functional Translation | Strategic Outcome |
|-----------------|------------------------|-------------------|
| **Utility** | Protection, preservation, handling convenience | Reduced damage costs, longer shelf life |
| **Uniqueness** | Differentiation through form, color, texture | Shelf stand-out, brand recall |
| **Universal Communication** | Conveys info, brand story, emotional cues | Builds trust, drives purchase |

## 1.4 Standard KTU Classification Metrics (Bold Constants)

The following standard metrics are universally applied in KTU board exam valuation:

- **Primary Packaging (Primary Container):** Holds the actual product — closest to the product itself. *Examples:* toothpaste tube, perfume bottle, smartphone box interior.
- **Secondary Packaging:** Wraps the primary package — used for merchandising, bundling, and shelf display. *Examples:* the cardboard box containing six Coke cans.
- **Transport Packaging (Tertiary):** Designed for warehousing, shipping, and distribution logistics. *Examples:* wooden pallets, corrugated shipping cartons, shrink wrap on palletized goods.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:**
> Students frequently confuse *secondary* packaging with *transport* packaging. **Secondary = Shelf-level. Tertiary = Warehouse/Logistics level.** Memorize the rule: *S-T-T = Shelf → Transport → Tertiary.*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Core Functions of Packaging (KTU High-Yield)

The KTU Product and Brand Management syllabus mandates a thorough understanding of the functional taxonomy of packaging. These seven functions are recurrently tested in 3-mark and 14-mark questions.

### 2.1.1 Functional Decomposition (Structured Logic)

**Step 1 — Containment & Protection (The Physical Role):**
- Protects product from mechanical shock, vibration, compression
- Shields against environmental degradation: moisture, oxygen, light, dust, microorganisms
- Prevents contamination, tampering, and leakage during transit

**Step 2 — Preservation & Shelf-Life Extension (The Biochemical Role):**
- Acts as a barrier against oxidation (O₂), humidity (H₂O vapor), and UV radiation
- In food/pharma: extends shelf life via Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP) and Vacuum Packaging
- *Example:* Tetra Pak's six-layer aseptic carton extends milk shelf life to 6 months without refrigeration

**Step 3 — Information Communication (The Cognitive Role):**
- Conveys mandatory disclosures (ingredients, nutritional info, MRP, batch, expiry)
- Communicates brand promise, usage instructions, warnings
- Acts as a "silent billboard" on crowded retail shelves

**Step 4 — Identification & Branding (The Identity Role):**
- Brand logo, color scheme, typography, mascot embedded in design
- Builds brand recognition and recall across product line extensions
- *Example:* Coca-Cola's contour bottle is recognizable even when shattered into fragments

**Step 5 — Convenience & Handling (The Ergonomic Role):**
- Ease of opening, resealing, pouring, dispensing, carrying
- Consumer-friendly features: tear notches, flip caps, ergonomic grips, single-serve pods

**Step 6 — Promotion & Sales (The Persuasion Role):**
- Attracts attention through visual aesthetics, structural uniqueness
- Influences impulse purchase decisions at Point of Sale (POS)
- *Industry statistic:* Approximately **70–80%** of consumer packaged goods (CPG) purchase decisions are made at the shelf

**Step 7 — Environmental & Disposal Role (The Sustainability Role):**
- Modern packaging must consider end-of-life recyclability, biodegradability, and circular economy compliance
- Drives brand image through sustainable choices (e.g., paper-based pouches, compostable cutlery)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Silent Salesman Doctrine:**
> In an uncluttered home-shopping scenario (e.g., Kirana stores, vending machines, e-commerce unboxing), the package itself must execute the sales pitch. This is the foundation of the *Point-of-Purchase (POP)* concept in KTU boards.

## 2.2 Packaging Materials — A Comparative Framework

| Material Category | Sub-Types | Key Properties | Typical Applications | KTU Board Relevance |
|------------------|-----------|----------------|----------------------|--------------------|
| **Paper & Paperboard** | Kraft paper, Corrugated board, Carton board | Recyclable, biodegradable, printable | Cereals, pharma cartons, e-commerce | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Glass** | Soda-lime, Borosilicate | Inert, premium feel, heavy, fragile | Beverages, perfumes, jams | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Metals** | Aluminum, Tin-plated steel (TFS), Tinplate | Impermeable, durable, recyclable | Soft drink cans, aerosol, milk powder | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Plastics** | PET, HDPE, LDPE, PP, PS, PVC, Bioplastics | Lightweight, versatile, moldable | Bottles, films, pouches, caps | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Composite / Multi-layer** | Tetra Pak, Aseptic packs, Blister packs | Combines barrier properties of multiple materials | UHT milk, pharma, juices | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Wood & Natural Fibers** | Wooden crates, jute, bamboo | Eco-friendly, rustic, premium | Wine, organic produce, artisanal goods | ⭐⭐ |
| **Flexible Packaging** | Pouches, sachets, films, laminates | Lightweight, low cost, high shelf efficiency | Snacks, condiments, detergents | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Metric:**
> The **"Plastic-to-Glass" weight ratio** for a 1-liter beverage container is approximately **1:8** — plastics are about **8 times lighter** than glass for equivalent capacity. This is a frequent KTU sub-question in 14-mark answers.

## 2.3 Packaging Design Considerations (KTU 7C Framework)

> [!TIP]
> The **7C Framework** is the high-yield KTU board tool for designing packaging. Every brand manager must audit each "C" before product launch.

- **C1 — Cut:** Structural geometry — shape, size, ergonomics
- **C2 — Color:** Color psychology — red = appetite (KFC, Maggi), green = eco/organic, blue = trust (Dettol, Nivea)
- **C3 — Composition:** Material composition — barrier layers, recyclability
- **C4 — Copy:** Text content — claims, ingredients, tagline, regulatory info
- **C5 — Character:** Brand personality — playful, premium, minimalist, traditional
- **C6 — Consistency:** Line-extension coherence — variants must share visual DNA (e.g., Parle-G gold pack family)
- **C7 — Compliance:** Legal & regulatory — FSSAI, BIS, Legal Metrology Act, Drug & Cosmetic Act

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Packaging Decision Equations

> [!NOTE]
> **No single mathematical formula dominates packaging as it does in engineering core subjects.** However, the KTU boards test *decision-making frameworks* and *cost-benefit equations* that can be expressed symbolically.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Shelf Impact Score (SIS)} &= w_1 \cdot V + w_2 \cdot C + w_3 \cdot R + w_4 \cdot D \\[4pt]
\text{Packaging ROI} &= \frac{(\Delta \text{Revenue} + \Delta \text{Brand Equity} - \text{Packaging Cost})}{\text{Packaging Cost}} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
\text{Unit Load Efficiency (ULE)} &= \frac{\text{Volume occupied by product}}{\text{Total secondary package volume}} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
\text{Logistics Cost Ratio (LCR)} &= \frac{\text{Cost of packaging}}{\text{Total landed cost of product}} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Where:**
- $V$ = Visibility score (0–10)
- $C$ = Color contrast score (0–10)
- $R$ = Recognition score (0–10)
- $D$ = Differentiation score (0–10)
- $w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4$ are weighted importance factors summing to **1.0**
- $\Delta$ = incremental change attributable to packaging redesign

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Character Alert (Markdown Compliance):**
> The vertical pipe symbol `|` is **strictly avoided** inside table rows. Use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX expressions. Students reproducing equations in markdown must follow this KTU 2024 digital submission protocol.

## 2.5 Real-World Strategic Utility

The 7C framework is not academic — global FMCG majors deploy it directly:

- **Apple Inc.:** Leverages Cut (minimalist geometry), Consistency (uniform white across SKUs), and Character (premium minimalist ethos)
- **Frooti:** Leverages Color (tropical green-yellow) and Copy (Hindi tagline — *"Jhakaas"* in regionalized variants)
- **Amul:** Leverages Consistency (Amul Girl mascot consistent across butter, cheese, chocolate milk)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Decision Matrices

## 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation: The Packaging Decision Tree

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Foundation:**
> The following step-by-step algorithm formalizes the KTU-board-tested decision-making sequence for selecting packaging for a new product launch. Each step must appear explicitly in 14-mark answers.

### Step 1 — Define the Product & Its Physical Characteristics

**Conversion Logic:**
The first logical operation is product characterization. Identify:
- Physical state: solid / liquid / gas / semi-solid
- Chemical sensitivity: oxidation-prone, hygroscopic, light-sensitive
- Shelf-life requirement (in days/months)
- Unit volume / unit weight / fragility index

**Mathematical Representation:**

$$
\text{Product Profile Vector} = (P_{\text{state}},\ P_{\text{sensitivity}},\ T_{\text{shelf}},\ V_{\text{unit}},\ W_{\text{unit}},\ F_{\text{fragility}})
$$

### Step 2 — Identify Target Market & Channel of Distribution

**Conversion Logic:**
Packaging must suit the channel. E-commerce requires **higher durability** (multiple touchpoints, courier handling); kirana requires **shelf visibility**; institutional/B2B requires **bulk efficiency**.

**Channel-Packaging Matrix:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Channel-Package Alignment} &= f(\text{Distribution Layer},\ \text{Consumer Touchpoint Frequency}) \\[4pt]
\text{E-commerce Packaging Index (EPI)} &= \alpha \cdot \text{Durability} + \beta \cdot \text{Unboxing Experience} + \gamma \cdot \text{Return Compatibility} \\[4pt]
\text{where } \alpha + \beta + \gamma &= 1.0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Establish the Functional Requirements

**Conversion Logic:**
Translate the product profile into packaging performance specs.

**Performance Specification Equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{required}} = \{ & \text{Barrier}_{O_2}, \ \text{Barrier}_{H_2O}, \ \text{Barrier}_{\text{Light}}, \\
& \text{Mechanical}_{\text{compression}}, \ \text{Mechanical}_{\text{impact}}, \ \text{Thermal}_{\text{stability}}, \\
& \text{Seal}_{\text{integrity}}, \ \text{Open}_{\text{ease}}, \ \text{Dispense}_{\text{rate}} \}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Generate Material Candidates

**Conversion Logic:**
Using the performance spec $P_{\text{required}}$, generate a candidate set of materials. Apply the **Material Selection Matrix**.

**Selection Scoring Formula:**

$$
M_i^{\text{score}} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot s_{ij}, \quad \text{where } \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j = 1.0
$$

Here:
- $M_i^{\text{score}}$ = Aggregate score of material candidate $i$
- $w_j$ = Importance weight of criterion $j$
- $s_{ij}$ = Score of material $i$ on criterion $j$ (scale 0–10)
- $n$ = Total number of criteria

### Step 5 — Iterate the Decision with Cost-Benefit Analysis

**Conversion Logic:**
For each shortlisted material, compute total cost of ownership including material, processing, logistics damage rate, and shelf-disposal cost.

**Total Cost of Packaging (TCP):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCP} &= (C_{\text{material}} \times Q) + (C_{\text{conversion}} \times Q) + (C_{\text{logistics damage}} \times Q) + (C_{\text{disposal/return}}) \\[4pt]
\text{where } Q &= \text{Annual Production Volume in Units}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Validate Against Regulatory Compliance

**Conversion Logic:**
Every package must comply with Indian regulations:
- **Legal Metrology Act, 2009** — declaration of MRP, net quantity, manufacturer address
- **FSSAI Packaging Regulations, 2018** — for food contact materials
- **Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940** — for pharmaceutical packaging
- **BIS Standards** — for specific material grades
- **E-Waste Management Rules** — for electronics packaging
- **Plastics Waste Management Rules** — for plastic carry bags and multilayered plastics

### Step 7 — Prototype, Test, and Iterate

**Conversion Logic:**
Conduct drop tests, vibration tests, compression tests, accelerated aging tests, and consumer eye-tracking studies.

**Test Performance Metric:**

$$
\text{Test Pass Rate (TPR)} = \frac{\text{Number of samples passing all tests}}{\text{Total samples tested}} \times 100\%
$$

A **TPR ≥ 95%** is the industry benchmark for product launch readiness.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Packaging Strategies by Product Lifecycle Stage

| Lifecycle Stage | Strategic Packaging Focus | Typical Material Shift | Brand Implication |
|-----------------|--------------------------|------------------------|-------------------|
| **Introduction** | Premium differentiation, information density, trial incentive | High-grade rigid (glass, metal, premium plastic) | Builds perceived quality, justifies introductory pricing |
| **Growth** | Consistency + line extensions, family branding | Balanced cost-performance, scale economics | Reinforces category leadership |
| **Maturity** | Cost optimization, format variants, convenience features | Shift to flexible pouches, single-serve sachets | Defends market share via format proliferation |
| **Decline** | Cost minimization, harvest materials | Cheapest viable substrate, recycled content | Preserves margin, signals category exit |
| **Revival/Re-launch** | Modernization, eco-redesign, premiumization | Sustainable, minimalist, premium bio-materials | Repositions brand to next-generation consumer |

## 3.3 Cost-Benefit Worked Example (Step-by-Step Exhaustive Derivation)

**Problem:**
A FMCG brand is launching a 250 ml fruit juice. Three packaging options are being evaluated. Determine the optimal option using a weighted multi-criteria decision matrix.

**Option Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Option A (Glass Bottle)} &: \text{Cost} = ₹8, \text{Durability} = 6, \text{Sustainability} = 9, \text{Shelf Appeal} = 9 \\
\text{Option B (PET Bottle)} &: \text{Cost} = ₹3, \text{Durability} = 7, \text{Sustainability} = 5, \text{Shelf Appeal} = 7 \\
\text{Option C (Tetra Pak)} &: \text{Cost} = ₹5, \text{Durability} = 9, \text{Sustainability} = 6, \text{Shelf Appeal} = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Weight Assignment (Strategic Priorities for Premium Juice):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{\text{Cost}} &= 0.20 \\
w_{\text{Durability}} &= 0.30 \\
w_{\text{Sustainability}} &= 0.20 \\
w_{\text{Shelf Appeal}} &= 0.30 \\
\sum w_j &= 1.00 \quad \text{(Sanity check satisfied)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Aggregate Score Computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}_A &= (0.20 \times 8) + (0.30 \times 6) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.30 \times 9) \\
&= 1.60 + 1.80 + 1.80 + 2.70 = 7.90 \\[4pt]
\text{Score}_B &= (0.20 \times 3) + (0.30 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 5) + (0.30 \times 7) \\
&= 0.60 + 2.10 + 1.00 + 2.10 = 5.80 \\[4pt]
\text{Score}_C &= (0.20 \times 5) + (0.30 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 6) + (0.30 \times 6) \\
&= 1.00 + 2.70 + 1.20 + 1.80 = 6.70
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:**

$$
\text{Score}_A = 7.90 > \text{Score}_C = 6.70 > \text{Score}_B = 5.80
$$

**Conclusion:** Option A (Glass Bottle) is optimal for a **premium** juice launch where shelf appeal and sustainability are weighted heavily. If the brand repositioned toward **mass-market affordability**, the weights would shift and Option B would dominate.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU Boards:**
> The optimal packaging is *not* an absolute — it is a **function of strategic weights**. Students who state the answer without justifying the weights typically lose 2–3 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Three-Level Packaging Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["PRODUCT: Actual physical good"]
    node2["PRIMARY PACKAGING: Direct product contact"]
    node3["SECONDARY PACKAGING: Shelf merchandising"]
    node4["TRANSPORT TERTIARY: Logistics and warehousing"]
    node5["Consumer Touchpoint: End user interface"]
    node6["Retail Shelf: Brand visibility layer"]
    node7["Distribution Channel: Transit protection"]
    node8["Function: Containment and protection"]
    node9["Function: Promotion and brand display"]
    node10["Function: Bulk handling and shipping"]
    node11["Example: Toothpaste tube inside carton"]
    node12["Example: Box of 6 beverage cans"]
    node13["Example: Wooden pallet with shrink wrap"]

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node2 -.-> node5
    node3 -.-> node6
    node4 -.-> node7
    node2 -.-> node8
    node3 -.-> node9
    node4 -.-> node10
    node2 -.-> node11
    node3 -.-> node12
    node4 -.-> node13
```

## 4.2 The 7C Packaging Design Framework — Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["Product Launch Trigger"]
    stepB["Step 1: Define Product Profile"]
    stepC["Step 2: Identify Channel and Market"]
    stepD["Step 3: Establish Functional Requirements"]
    stepE["Step 4: Apply 7C Design Audit"]
    stepF["Cut Geometry and ergonomics"]
    stepG["Color Psychology alignment"]
    stepH["Composition Material selection"]
    stepI["Copy Information hierarchy"]
    stepJ["Character Brand personality"]
    stepK["Consistency Line coherence"]
    stepL["Compliance Regulatory check"]
    stepM["Prototype Test and Iterate"]
    stepN["Launch Ready Decision"]

    stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepE --> stepG
    stepE --> stepH
    stepE --> stepI
    stepE --> stepJ
    stepE --> stepK
    stepE --> stepL
    stepL --> stepM --> stepN
```

## 4.3 Packaging Strategy — Product Lifecycle Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    phase1["INTRODUCTION: Premium differentiation, rigid materials, dense info"]
    phase2["GROWTH: Family consistency, line extensions, balanced cost"]
    phase3["MATURITY: Format proliferation, single serve, flexible pouches"]
    phase4["DECLINE: Cost minimization, recycled content, harvest mode"]
    phase5["REVIVAL: Eco redesign, premiumization, modernized identity"]

    phase1 --> phase2 --> phase3 --> phase4
    phase4 --> phase5
    phase5 -.-> phase2
```

## 4.4 Sustainable Packaging — Circular Economy Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rawA["Raw Material Sourcing: Bio based or recycled inputs"]
    rawB["Design Phase: Minimal material, mono material, recyclable"]
    rawC["Manufacturing: Low carbon footprint, energy efficient"]
    rawD["Distribution: Optimized transport, lightweight"]
    rawE["Consumer Use: Reusable, refillable, durable"]
    rawF["Collection: Reverse logistics, take back schemes"]
    rawG["Recycling: Material recovery, reprocessing"]
    rawH["Composting: Organic decomposition for bio plastics"]
    rawI["Circular Re entry: Feedstock into raw material sourcing"]

    rawA --> rawB --> rawC --> rawD --> rawE --> rawF
    rawF --> rawG
    rawF --> rawH
    rawG --> rawI
    rawH --> rawI
    rawI -.-> rawA
```

## 4.5 Strategic Block Diagram — Packaging Decision Matrix Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inp1["Inputs: Product specs, market data, cost targets"]
    inp2["Step A: Material candidate generation"]
    inp3["Step B: Multi criteria scoring on cost durability sustainability appeal"]
    inp4["Step C: Weighted aggregate score computation"]
    inp5["Step D: Sensitivity analysis on weights"]
    inp6["Step E: Regulatory compliance audit"]
    inp7["Step F: Prototype validation via accelerated testing"]
    inp8["Output: Final packaging specification and supplier sign off"]

    inp1 --> inp2 --> inp3 --> inp4 --> inp5 --> inp6 --> inp7 --> inp8
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Define the term "Product Packaging" as a marketing tool. Explain any two strategic functions of packaging with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Product packaging is the coordinated system of designing and producing the container, wrapper, label, and physical appearance of a product to perform protective, informational, promotional, and logistical functions. It is recognized in modern marketing as the "Fifth P" of the marketing mix and as a "silent salesman" at the point of sale.

**Function 1 — Promotion and Sales (with example):**
Packaging attracts consumer attention at the shelf and influences impulse purchase. *Example:* Coca-Cola's contour bottle and signature red color create instant recognition, contributing to its **70%+** global brand recall.

**Function 2 — Product Protection and Preservation (with example):**
Packaging acts as a barrier against environmental degradation. *Example:* Tetra Pak's six-layer aseptic carton protects UHT milk from oxygen, light, and microbial contamination, enabling **6 months of ambient shelf life** without refrigeration.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for crisp definition, 1 mark each for the two functions with example. Total = 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between Primary, Secondary, and Transport packaging with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Level | Definition | Example |
|-------|-----------|---------|
| **Primary Packaging** | Directly contains the product; in immediate contact with the product | Amul Butter wrapper (200 g pack) |
| **Secondary Packaging** | Wraps multiple primary packs for shelf merchandising and bundling | Cardboard carton holding 24 Maggi noodle packets |
| **Transport (Tertiary) Packaging** | Used for warehousing, bulk handling, and shipping | Wooden pallet with shrink-wrapped corrugated cases of Bisleri water |

*(1 mark each for correct identification + 1 mark for example set. Total = 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> **Common mark-loss points in 14-mark packaging answers:**
> 1. **No diagram or visual support** → loses 2 marks. Always include a labeled 3-level packaging diagram.
> 2. **Mere listing of functions without examples** → loses up to 4 marks. Every function MUST have a real-world FMCG or durable example.
> 3. **Ignoring the Indian regulatory framework** (FSSAI, Legal Metrology Act, BIS) → loses 2–3 marks, as KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes NEP 2020 outcome-based learning tied to indigenous context.
> 4. **No mention of sustainability** → loses 2 marks. KTU 2024 boards heavily weight the *Green Packaging* and *Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR)* concepts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain the seven functions of product packaging in detail. How does packaging act as a "silent salesman" at the point of sale? **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** Discuss the various factors influencing packaging design decisions. Illustrate with reference to a branded FMCG product of your choice. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

Packaging performs seven integrated functions, each serving distinct strategic purposes:

**1. Containment and Protection:** The foundational function — packaging must contain the product securely and shield it from mechanical, environmental, and biological damage. *Example:* Corrugated boxes with EPE foam inserts protect smartphones during courier transit.

**2. Preservation and Shelf-Life Extension:** Multi-layer barrier structures block oxygen, moisture, and UV. *Example:* Six-layer Tetra Pak extends UHT milk shelf life to **6 months** at ambient temperature.

**3. Information Communication:** Packages carry mandatory disclosures (MRP, ingredients, batch number, expiry) and persuasive brand copy. *Example:* FSSAI-compliant nutrition table on Kellogg's muesli packs.

**4. Identification and Branding:** Visual elements — logo, color, typography, mascot — make the brand instantly recognizable. *Example:* Amul Girl mascot is consistent across butter, cheese, and ice cream.

**5. Convenience and Handling:** Ergonomic design supports ease of opening, resealing, dispensing, and carrying. *Example:* Flip-top cap on Dabur Honey jar, single-serve Nespresso pods.

**6. Promotion and Sales — The Silent Salesman Doctrine:** Approximately **70–80%** of FMCG purchase decisions are made at the shelf. Packaging must communicate value, quality, and relevance within **3–7 seconds** of consumer attention. *Example:* Maggi's iconic yellow-and-red pack triggers appetite cues and evokes nostalgia, enabling impulse purchase without salesperson intervention.

**7. Environmental and Disposal Role:** Modern packaging integrates recyclability, biodegradability, and EPR compliance. *Example:* ITC's "Plastic Positive" initiative and Sunfeast's mono-material recyclable pouches.

The silent salesman role is most evident in self-service retail environments — modern trade, e-commerce, vending machines — where no salesperson interacts with the buyer. The package must independently (i) attract attention, (ii) communicate value proposition, (iii) build trust, and (iv) prompt purchase.

#### Model Solution for (b):

Packaging design is influenced by a constellation of interconnected factors. Illustrating with **Maggi Noodles (2-Minute)** by Nestlé India:

**1. Product Characteristics:** Maggi is a dry, hygroscopic product requiring moisture and oxygen barrier. Hence, the multi-layer laminate (PET/ALU/PP) ensures crispness and 9-month shelf life.

**2. Marketing Considerations:** Target audience is young consumers and mothers — the playful yellow-and-red color palette triggers appetite and youth appeal. Brand mascot "Maggi Girl" reinforces family-friendly positioning.

**3. Cost Constraints:** Maggi must be accessible at ₹10–12 price points, necessitating efficient material use. Nestlé India has progressively optimized laminate thickness without compromising barrier properties.

**4. Channel of Distribution:** Sold across modern trade, traditional kirana, and e-commerce — the pack must withstand rough handling, varied shelf conditions, and drop tests.

**5. Regulatory Compliance:** Pack carries FSSAI logo, MRP, ingredient declaration, nutritional info, batch code, manufacturing date, expiry, vegetarian mark (green dot), and allergen warnings — all mandated by the Legal Metrology Act, 2009 and FSSAI Packaging Regulations, 2018.

**6. Environmental Sustainability:** Under Plastic Waste Management (Amendment) Rules, 2022, Nestlé has committed to mono-material recyclable packs and EPR collection of multilayered plastic waste.

**7. Competitive Differentiation:** Maggi's red "Maggi" wordmark, signature "Maggi-M" symbol, and consistent pack architecture across variants (Masala, Atta, Chicken) defend against competition from Top Ramen, Yippee, and Patanjali.

**8. Technology and Innovation:** The pack incorporates easy-tear notch, zip-lock resealability for family packs, and microwave-safe variants — all reflecting consumer convenience expectations.

**Conclusion:** Packaging design at Maggi exemplifies the **convergence of product engineering, marketing strategy, regulatory compliance, and sustainability commitments**, validating the 7C framework.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** What is sustainable packaging? Discuss the key drivers and challenges of adopting green packaging in the Indian FMCG industry. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** Explain the role of labeling in product packaging. Discuss the mandatory and voluntary elements of a product label with reference to Indian regulatory framework. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition:**
Sustainable (or "green") packaging refers to packaging solutions that minimize environmental impact across the entire lifecycle — from raw material sourcing to end-of-life disposal — while meeting functional, economic, and brand-equity requirements. It prioritizes the **3Rs hierarchy — Reduce, Reuse, Recycle** — and increasingly incorporates bio-based, compostable, and mono-material designs.

**Key Drivers:**

1. **Regulatory Push:** India's Plastic Waste Management Rules (2016, amended 2022) mandate Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR), requiring brand owners to collect and recycle a defined percentage of their plastic packaging.

2. **Consumer Awareness:** Gen Z and Millennial consumers increasingly prefer eco-conscious brands. A 2023 McKinsey survey showed **60%+** of urban Indian consumers willing to pay a premium for sustainable packaging.

3. **Brand Equity:** Sustainability is now a brand differentiator. Companies like ITC, Tata, and HUL leverage sustainability narratives to command premium positioning.

4. **Cost Optimization:** Lightweighting and material efficiency reduce logistics costs and carbon footprint, indirectly improving margins.

5. **Global Trade Compliance:** Export to EU requires compliance with EU Packaging and Packaging Waste Regulation (PPWR) and Single-Use Plastics Directive.

**Key Challenges:**

1. **Technical Limitations:** Many sustainable materials (e.g., biodegradable PLA) lack the barrier properties of conventional multi-layer laminates — particularly for food and pharma.

2. **Cost Premium:** Recyclable and bio-based materials are typically **15–30% more expensive** than conventional plastic.

3. **Collection Infrastructure:** India's municipal solid waste segregation and recycling infrastructure is fragmented, undermining circularity.

4. **Consumer Behavior:** Despite stated preferences, consumer compliance with segregation is low, requiring intensive awareness campaigns.

5. **Performance Trade-offs:** Mono-material designs can compromise shelf life, especially for moisture-sensitive products.

**Indian Industry Examples:** Amul's recyclable butter wrappers, ITC's PaperBoards-based packs, Tata Sampann's compostable pouches, and Dabur's recycled PET (rPET) bottles.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Definition of Labeling:**
A label is the carrier of textual, graphical, and symbolic information affixed to or printed on the package. It serves identification, descriptive, promotional, and legal-compliance functions.

**Mandatory Elements (under Indian Law):**

1. **Legal Metrology Act, 2009:**
   - Maximum Retail Price (MRP) — inclusive of all taxes
   - Net quantity (weight, volume, count) in standard units
   - Manufacturer's name and full address
   - Country of origin ("Made in India")
   - Customer care contact details

2. **FSSAI Regulations (for food products):**
   - FSSAI logo and license number
   - Ingredient list in descending order of quantity
   - Nutritional information per 100 g / 100 ml
   - Veg / Non-veg symbol (green/brown dot)
   - Allergen declarations
   - Best before / Use by date
   - Batch / Lot identification
   - Storage instructions
   - FSSAI-compliant font sizes (min 1 mm for declaration)

3. **Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940 (for pharma):**
   - Drug composition, dosage, warnings
   - Batch number, manufacturing date, expiry date
   - "Rx" / "OTC" classification marking

4. **Consumer Protection Act, 2019:**
   - No false or misleading claims
   - No deceptive packaging (e.g., oversized containers with hollow interiors)

**Voluntary Elements (Marketing-driven):**

1. Brand name, logo, tagline, mascot
2. Promotional offers ("Buy 1 Get 1 Free")
3. QR codes linking to traceability or marketing microsites
4. Nutritional claims ("High Protein", "Low Fat")
5. Certifications (ISI mark, Agmark, India Organic, FSSAI Organic)
6. Sustainability claims ("100% Recyclable", "Plastic Negative")

**Integration with Brand Strategy:**
A label must balance regulatory compliance with brand storytelling. *Example:* Paper Boat's label integrates Hindi/Hinglish storytelling with mandatory declarations, achieving both legal compliance and emotional brand-building.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Product Packaging (KTU Module 2):**

### Core Definitions
- ☐ Packaging = container + wrapper + label + visual design (AMA definition)

- ☐ Secondary = shelf merchandising
- ☐ Tertiary = transport / logistics

### Seven Functions (Mnemonic: **C-P-I-I-C-P-E**)
- ☐ **C**ontainment
- ☐ **P**reservation
- ☐ **I**nformation
- ☐ **I**dentification/Branding
- ☐ **C**onvenience
- ☐ **P**romotion (silent salesman)
- ☐ **E**nvironmental/Sustainability

### Design Framework
- ☐ **7C Framework:** Cut, Color, Composition, Copy, Character, Consistency, Compliance

### Material Categories (Priority Order for Board Exams)
- ☐ Paper & Paperboard
- ☐ Plastics (PET, HDPE, PP, LDPE)
- ☐ Glass
- ☐ Metals (Aluminum, Tinplate)

### Key Indian Regulations
- ☐ Legal Metrology Act, 2009

- ☐ Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940
- ☐ BIS Standards
- ☐ Consumer Protection Act, 2019 (anti-dark-pattern packaging)

### Strategic Frameworks
- ☐ Packaging Strategy by Product Lifecycle Stage (Introduction → Growth → Maturity → Decline → Revival)
- ☐ Multi-Criteria Decision Matrix with weighted scoring
- ☐ Circular Economy: Reduce-Reuse-Recycle hierarchy

### Sustainability & Green Packaging
- ☐ 3Rs Hierarchy
- ☐ Mono-material design, light-weighting, refillable formats
- ☐ EPR obligations for brand owners

### High-Yield Numerical Trivia
- ☐ **70–80%** of FMCG purchase decisions made at the shelf
- ☐ Plastic : Glass weight ratio for 1L = **1 : 8**
- ☐ Tetra Pak shelf life for UHT milk = **6 months** ambient
- ☐ Consumer attention window at shelf = **3–7 seconds**
- ☐ Sustainable packaging cost premium = **15–30%**

### Board-Exam Critical Examples (Memorize)

- ☐ Maggi yellow-red pack architecture

- ☐ Apple minimalist white box
