Self-Respect"] L4 --> L4A["Behavioral Loyalty"] L4 --> L4B["Attitudinal Attachment"] L4 --> L4C["Sense of Community"] L4 --> L4D["Active Engagement"] ``` ## 4.3 CBBE Process Flow — From Marketing Inputs to Customer Outcomes ```mermaid flowchart LR MKT["Marketing Inputs
Product, Price, Place, Promotion"] AW["Brand Awareness
Recognition + Recall"] IMG["Brand Image
Performance + Imagery Associations"] JUDG["Customer Judgments
Rational Evaluation"] FEEL["Customer Feelings
Emotional Response"] RES["Brand Resonance
Loyalty + Advocacy + Community"] MKT --> AW MKT --> IMG AW --> JUDG IMG --> JUDG AW --> FEEL IMG --> FEEL JUDG --> RES FEEL --> RES ``` ## 4.4 Sources of CBBE — Decision Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Is the Brand KNOWN to the customer?"] KnownYES["Customer has Brand Awareness
Recognizes and Recalls brand"] KnownNO["No CBBE Possible
Brand is unknown"] Familiar["Are associations FAVORABLE?"] Strong["Are associations STRONG?"] Unique["Are associations UNIQUE?"] PositiveEquity["POSITIVE CBBE ACHIEVED
Favorable + Strong + Unique"] WeakEquity["Weak or Negative CBBE
Associations missing one or more attributes"] Start --> KnownYES Start --> KnownNO KnownYES --> Familiar Familiar --> Strong Strong --> Unique Unique --> PositiveEquity Unique --> WeakEquity ``` # 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define Customer-Based Brand Equity (CBBE). Mention its two core components.** **Model Answer:** Customer-Based Brand Equity (CBBE) is the differential effect that brand knowledge has on consumer response to the marketing of that brand. It exists when consumers are familiar with the brand and hold favorable, strong, and unique brand associations in memory. The **two core components** are: 1. **Brand Awareness** – The ability of customers to recognize and recall the brand. 2. **Brand Image** – The perceptions and associations linked to the brand in the consumer's mind. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write only *"brand awareness"* as the definition. CBBE = Awareness + Image **combined**. Missing one will cost you 1.5 marks. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **List the four levels of Keller's Brand Resonance Pyramid in the correct order.** **Model Answer:** The four levels of Keller's Brand Resonance Pyramid, in ascending order, are: 1. **Brand Identity** (Brand Salience) 2. **Brand Meaning** (Performance + Imagery) 3. **Brand Responses** (Judgments + Feelings) 4. **Brand Resonance** (Relationships) The order is critical — each level is a prerequisite for the next. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer **ONE question out of two** in this slot, carrying 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. --- ### **📌 QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]** **Q. (a)** Explain the concept of Customer-Based Brand Equity (CBBE) as defined by Kevin Lane Keller. Discuss in detail the four steps involved in building a strong brand using the CBBE model. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Introduction (1.5 marks):** Kevin Lane Keller's CBBE model views brand equity as residing in the **minds of consumers**. It is the differential effect of brand knowledge on consumer response to marketing. The model is built as a **four-step pyramid** where each level must be established before moving to the next. **Step 1: Brand Identity — Brand Salience (1.5 marks):** - Refers to the **depth** (ease of recall) and **breadth** (range of usage situations) of brand awareness. - The brand must be easily recognized and recalled by customers in appropriate contexts. - Example: Airtel's "Doosra number" campaign enhanced salience. **Step 2: Brand Meaning (1.5 marks):** Divided into two categories: - **Performance:** Functional benefits of the product (reliability, durability, features). - **Imagery:** Psychological and social benefits (user profile, brand personality, heritage). - Example: Volvo = Performance (safety) + Imagery (caring, family-oriented). **Step 3: Brand Responses (1.5 marks):** - **Brand Judgments (rational):** Customer's opinions — quality, credibility, consideration, superiority. - **Brand Feelings (emotional):** Emotional reactions — warmth, fun, excitement, security, social approval. - Example: Apple Judgments = "innovative"; Apple Feelings = "creative, cool." **Step 4: Brand Resonance (1 mark):** - The pinnacle — deepest psychological bonding with the brand. - Includes behavioral loyalty, attitudinal attachment, sense of community, and active engagement. - Example: Harley-Davidson Owners Group. **Conclusion (0.5 marks):** A brand that successfully climbs all four levels achieves **strong, positive, and sustainable CBBE**, leading to premium pricing, brand advocacy, and competitive immunity. --- **(b)** Differentiate between **Brand Awareness** and **Brand Image**. Explain how these two together form the foundation of CBBE. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Brand Awareness | Brand Image | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Customer's ability to recall or recognize the brand | Customer's perceptions and associations about the brand | | **Nature** | Cognitive (recognition-based) | Perceptual and emotional | | **Core Question** | "Do I know this brand?" | "What do I think/feel about this brand?" | | **Components** | Recognition, Recall (aided/unaided), Top-of-mind | Performance, Imagery, Judgments, Feelings | | **Measurement** | Aided/unaided recall tests, TOMA scores | Likert-scale association tests, projective techniques | | **Time to Build** | Relatively short (advertising-driven) | Long-term (experience and consistency-driven) | **How They Form the Foundation of CBBE (3 marks):** - CBBE mathematically equals the **union** of brand awareness and brand image in customer memory: $BK = BA \cup BI$. - Without **awareness**, a brand cannot enter the consumer's consideration set — there is **no equity** to talk about. - Without **favorable image**, awareness alone results in customers who *know* the brand but won't *buy* it. - Together, they enable the **differential effect** — consumers respond more favorably to branded products than to identical unbranded products. - Example: A new KTU student choosing a laptop — they are *aware* of both HP and ASUS (Awareness), but *prefer* HP because of perceived reliability and service (Image) → CBBE for HP > ASUS in their mind. --- ### **📌 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]** **Q. (a)** What is the **Brand Resonance Pyramid**? With a suitable diagram, explain each level and the brand-building blocks associated with each level. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Introduction (1 mark):** The **Brand Resonance Pyramid**, developed by Kevin Lane Keller, is a four-step model for building customer-based brand equity. It conceptualizes brand building as a **sequential process** where each stage establishes the foundation for the next. **Level 1 — Brand Identity: Salience (1.5 marks):** - Building Block: **Brand Salience**. - Identifies the brand and establishes its presence in the customer's mind. - Focus: Awareness through recognition and recall. **Level 2 — Brand Meaning (2 marks):** - Building Blocks: **Performance** and **Imagery**. - Performance = functional/utility benefits. - Imagery = psychological/social/abstract benefits. - Answers: "What is this brand fundamentally about?" **Level 3 — Brand Responses (1.5 marks):** - Building Blocks: **Brand Judgments** and **Brand Feelings**. - Judgments = cognitive responses (opinions, quality perception). - Feelings = emotional responses (warmth, excitement, security). - Answers: "What do customers think and feel about the brand?" **Level 4 — Brand Resonance (1 mark):** - Building Block: **Brand Relationships**. - The deepest level — psychological bonding and advocacy. - Components: Behavioral loyalty, attitudinal attachment, sense of community, active engagement. *Diagram credit is awarded for correctly drawing the four-level pyramid in a logical bottom-to-top sequence with labeled building blocks.* --- **(b)** Discuss the **sources of Brand Equity** with examples. How does a company like Apple use CBBE principles to maintain premium pricing? **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Sources of Brand Equity (3 marks):** 1. **Brand Awareness** – Created through consistent advertising, distinctive logos, and memorable taglines. 2. **Brand Image** – Built through product performance, brand personality, customer experience, and brand storytelling. 3. **Brand Loyalty** – Resulting from emotional connection and consistent value delivery. 4. **Brand Associations** – Anything the customer links to the brand (e.g., "Volvo = Safety"). 5. **Perceived Quality** – The customer's overall assessment of the brand's excellence. **Apple's Application of CBBE (4 marks):** - **Brand Identity:** The bitten apple logo is one of the most recognized globally → high **salience**. - **Brand Meaning — Performance:** Sleek, fast, reliable hardware + seamless iOS ecosystem. - **Brand Meaning — Imagery:** Apple is positioned as creative, innovative, premium — appealing to professionals and artists. - **Brand Responses — Judgments:** Customers perceive Apple as high-quality, premium, and worth the price. - **Brand Responses — Feelings:** Owning an Apple product evokes feelings of pride, status, and belonging to an elite group. - **Brand Resonance:** Apple users form a community (e.g., #ShotOniPhone), show high attitudinal attachment, and actively defend the brand online. **Outcome:** Despite charging **2x–3x the price** of competitor products, Apple maintains industry-leading customer loyalty and revenue per customer — a direct result of **strong, positive CBBE**. --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Writing the pyramid in the wrong order** → Lose 2 marks instantly. Always bottom-to-top: Identity → Meaning → Responses → Resonance. > 2. **Confusing Brand Image with Brand Identity** → Image = *customer's perception*; Identity = *brand's designed persona*. They are different. > 3. **Forgetting the "Differential Effect"** in CBBE definition → This is the **core academic phrase** in the syllabus. Missing it costs 1 mark. > 4. **Mixing up Judgments and Feelings** → Judgments = *head* (rational), Feelings = *heart* (emotional). Examiners specifically look for this distinction. > 5. **Skipping Indian/Real-world examples** → Use brands like Tata, Amul, Maggi, iPhone for higher marks. Generic "Coke vs. Pepsi" answers fetch average marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:** - ✅ **CBBE = Brand Knowledge (Awareness + Image)** in the customer's mind. - ✅ **Differential Effect** = $R_{branded} - R_{unbranded}$ is the mathematical core of CBBE. - ✅ **Pyramid Order (Bottom → Top):** Identity → Meaning → Responses → Resonance. - ✅ **Brand Identity = Salience** (Depth + Breadth of Awareness). - ✅ **Brand Meaning = Performance + Imagery.** - ✅ **Brand Responses = Judgments (Head) + Feelings (Heart).** - ✅ **Brand Resonance = Loyalty + Attachment + Community + Engagement.** - ✅ **Positive CBBE requires FAVORABLE + STRONG + UNIQUE** associations. - ✅ **Keller's model** is the **gold standard** CBBE framework in KTU syllabus. - ✅ **CBBE lives in the mind of the customer**, not in the product or factory. - ✅ **Order matters** — skipping a pyramid level breaks the equity build-up. - ✅ **Practical examples** (Tata, Amul, Apple, Volvo) score higher than theoretical-only answers. - ✅ **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** is a common proxy metric for measuring brand resonance. - ✅ **Top-of-Mind Awareness (TOMA)** is the gold standard measure of brand salience. - ✅ **Two-way arrow logic:** Marketing → Brand Knowledge → Customer Response → CBBE. --- > 🎓 **End of Module 3 Notes — Customer-Based Brand Equity** > *Aligned with PEPET753 (Product and Brand Management), KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education Framework.*

topics.title: Brand Switching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET753 - PRODUCT AND BRAND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Brand Switching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Brand Switching** is the consumer behavior phenomenon in which a buyer discontinues the purchase of a brand they have been consistently using and shifts their patronage to a competing brand, product category alternative, or substitute offering within a defined purchase cycle.

In the formal lexicon of **Consumer Behavior** and **Brand Equity Management**, brand switching is treated as the inverse construct of **Brand Loyalty**. While loyalty represents *stability of preference*, switching represents *volatility of preference*. KTU examiners typically frame brand switching as a function of three measurable dimensions:

| Dimension | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Switching Rate** | The percentage of customers who defect from Brand A to another brand within a specified period. |
| **Switching Intent** | The pre-purchase cognitive disposition of a consumer to try a different brand. |
| **Switching Cost** | The perceived (and real) economic, psychological, and time-based barriers a customer must overcome to switch. |

The standard marketing equation governing this behavior is:

$$BS_t = f(\text{Dissatisfaction}_{t-1}, \text{Competitor Attraction}_t, \text{Switching Cost}, \text{Innovation Seeking})$$

Where $BS_t$ represents the brand switching decision at time $t$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition 🧠

> [!NOTE]
> **The Tea Stall Analogy** ☕
>
> Imagine you are a student at KTU who has been buying *chai* from **Sharma Tea Stall** every morning for two years. One day, you notice a new stall — **Kerala Cafe** — has opened nearby, offering a free biscuit with every cup. Curious, you try it. The taste is good. You start going there twice a week, then eventually shift entirely.
>
> This behavioral shift — from a habitual brand (Sharma) to a trial-based shift (Kerala Cafe) — is exactly what brand switching represents. The two-year loyalty was disrupted by **attraction (freebie + novelty)**, and the **switching cost** was practically zero (both stalls cost the same and are equally close).

**Other intuitive examples:**
- Airtel → Jio mobile subscribers post-2016 (driven by price disruption).
- iPhone users switching to Samsung (driven by feature innovation).
- A consumer shifting from Coca-Cola to a local soda brand (driven by health perception).

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology Anchors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Frequency Terms You Must Memorize:**
> - **Brand Defection** – Permanent departure from a brand.
> - **Brand Switching Behavior (BSB)** – The aggregate pattern of consumers migrating between brands.
> - **Variety Seeking** – Switching driven by the *desire for novelty*, not dissatisfaction.
> - **Switching Barriers** – Factors preventing switch (loyalty programs, contracts, emotional bonds).
> - **BTS (Brand Switching)** – Standard three-letter abbreviation used in KTU questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Brand Switching

Brand switching in the **KTU Product & Brand Management** syllabus is structurally analyzed through three pillars:

### 🔹 Pillar 1: Reasons (Why customers switch)

1. **Push Factors (Dissatisfaction-driven)**
   - Poor product performance
   - Bad customer service
   - Price hikes without added value
   - Quality inconsistency

2. **Pull Factors (Attraction-driven)**
   - Superior competitor offering
   - Aggressive competitor pricing
   - Innovative features
   - Better brand communication

3. **Pry Factors (Habit-breaking forces)**
   - Life-stage changes (marriage, relocation)
   - Economic shifts
   - Information overload
   - Recommendation from peers

### 🔹 Pillar 2: Types of Brand Switching

| Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Voluntary Switching** | Customer chooses to switch without external pressure | Moving from Adidas to Nike |
| **Involuntary Switching** | Forced by external factors | Mandatory telecom shift after license cancellation |
| **Variety-Seeking Switching** | Driven by novelty | Trying a new shampoo every 3 months |
| **Price-Driven Switching** | Triggered by cost differentials | Switching between grocery brands during sales |
| **Service-Driven Switching** | Caused by service failures | Leaving a bank due to poor grievance redressal |

### 🔹 Pillar 3: Switching Barriers (Defensive Mechanisms for Brands)

- **Economic Switching Costs** – Cancellation fees, learning curve costs.
- **Psychological Switching Costs** – Emotional attachment, brand love.
- **Effort-Based Costs** – Time to set up new service, re-learning.
- **Loyalty Programs** – Reward points, tiered memberships.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet 🧮

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **exam-critical** and frequently appear in KTU Part B (14-mark) numericals.

### Table 2.1: Essential Brand Switching Equations

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Switching Rate (SR)** | $SR = \dfrac{N_s}{N_t} \times 100$ | $N_s$ = customers who switched, $N_t$ = total customers |
| 2 | **Retention Rate (RR)** | $RR = \dfrac{N_t - N_s}{N_t} \times 100$ | Percentage of customers retained |
| 3 | **Loyalty Index (LI)** | $LI = \dfrac{\text{Repeat Purchases}}{\text{Total Purchases}}$ | Closer to 1 = higher loyalty |
| 4 | **Defection Rate** | $DR = 1 - RR$ | Complement of retention rate |
| 5 | **Net Switching Score** | $NSS = \text{Gain from Switchers} - \text{Loss to Competitors}$ | Brand's net movement |
| 6 | **NBD Model (Ehrenberg)** | $P(r) = \dfrac{\binom{r + k - 1}{r} \cdot \binom{M - k}{T - r}}{\binom{M}{T}}$ | Probability of $r$ repeat buys |
| 7 | **Markov Transition Probability** | $P_{ij} = \dfrac{n_{ij}}{\sum_j n_{ij}}$ | Probability of moving from brand $i$ to $j$ |
| 8 | **Steady-State Market Share** | $\pi_j = \sum_i \pi_i \cdot P_{ij}$ | Long-run equilibrium share |
| 9 | **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** | $CLV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{(R_t - C_t)}{(1+d)^t}$ | Discounted future cash flow |
| 10 | **Switching Cost Index** | $SCI = w_1 EC + w_2 PC + w_3 LC$ | Weighted barrier cost composite |

---

## 2.3 The Markov Chain Brand Switching Model

The most widely tested analytical model in KTU 2024 Scheme is the **Discrete-Time Markov Chain (DTMC)** for brand switching.

**Assumptions:**
1. Brand choice at time $t+1$ depends **only** on the brand chosen at time $t$ (memoryless property).
2. Transition probabilities are **stationary** (constant over time).
3. The brand set is **finite** and exhaustive.

Let $P$ denote the **Transition Probability Matrix**:

$$
P = \begin{bmatrix} p_{11} & p_{12} & \cdots & p_{1n} \\ p_{21} & p_{22} & \cdots & p_{2n} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ p_{n1} & p_{n2} & \cdots & p_{nn} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Where each row $i$ sums to **1**:
$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} p_{ij} = 1 \quad \forall i$$

The market share at period $t$ is given by:
$$S_t = S_{t-1} \cdot P$$

And the **steady-state (long-run)** market share $\pi$ is the eigenvector of $P^T$ corresponding to eigenvalue 1:
$$\pi \cdot P = \pi \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_j \pi_j = 1$$

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of Brand Switching Analysis

Although brand switching is a marketing construct, its analytical machinery (Markov chains, NBD models, transition matrices) is fundamentally **stochastic processes engineering** applied to consumer choice. Industries employing this framework:

- **Telecom** – Subscriber migration modeling (Jio disrupted Vodafone-Idea).
- **Banking** – Account-holder retention analytics.
- **Automotive** – Loyalty and conquest modeling for next purchase cycle.
- **E-commerce** – Cart abandonment → competitor switching prediction.
- **SaaS** – Churn modeling for subscription products.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem Context:** A KTU study tracks 1,000 shampoo buyers in Kerala across two purchase periods. In Period 1, 600 bought Brand A, 300 bought Brand B, and 100 bought Brand C. The following transitions were observed:

| From \ To | A | B | C | Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 450 | 100 | 50 | 600 |
| **B** | 80 | 180 | 40 | 300 |
| **C** | 20 | 30 | 50 | 100 |

**Required:** (i) Transition probability matrix (ii) Market share in Period 2 (iii) Steady-state shares (iv) Switching rates for each brand.

---

### Step (i): Transition Probability Matrix

We compute $P_{ij} = \dfrac{n_{ij}}{\sum_j n_{ij}}$.

**For Row A:**
$$P_{AA} = \frac{450}{600} = 0.75$$
$$P_{AB} = \frac{100}{600} \approx 0.167$$
$$P_{AC} = \frac{50}{600} \approx 0.083$$

**For Row B:**
$$P_{BA} = \frac{80}{300} \approx 0.267$$
$$P_{BB} = \frac{180}{300} = 0.60$$
$$P_{BC} = \frac{40}{300} \approx 0.133$$

**For Row C:**
$$P_{CA} = \frac{20}{100} = 0.20$$
$$P_{CB} = \frac{30}{100} = 0.30$$
$$P_{CC} = \frac{50}{100} = 0.50$$

$$
P = \begin{bmatrix} 0.75 & 0.167 & 0.083 \\ 0.267 & 0.60 & 0.133 \\ 0.20 & 0.30 & 0.50 \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

### Step (ii): Market Share in Period 2

The Period 1 share vector is:
$$S_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 600 & 300 & 100 \end{bmatrix} / 1000 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.60 & 0.30 & 0.10 \end{bmatrix}$$

Applying $S_2 = S_1 \cdot P$:

$$
S_{2A} = (0.60)(0.75) + (0.30)(0.267) + (0.10)(0.20)
$$
$$
S_{2A} = 0.45 + 0.0801 + 0.02 = 0.5501
$$

$$
S_{2B} = (0.60)(0.167) + (0.30)(0.60) + (0.10)(0.30)
$$
$$
S_{2B} = 0.1002 + 0.18 + 0.03 = 0.3102
$$

$$
S_{2C} = (0.60)(0.083) + (0.30)(0.133) + (0.10)(0.50)
$$
$$
S_{2C} = 0.0498 + 0.0399 + 0.05 = 0.1397
$$

So $S_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5501 & 0.3102 & 0.1397 \end{bmatrix}$.

---

### Step (iii): Steady-State Market Share

We solve $\pi \cdot P = \pi$, i.e., $\pi \cdot (P - I) = 0$.

$$
(P - I) = \begin{bmatrix} -0.25 & 0.167 & 0.083 \\ 0.267 & -0.40 & 0.133 \\ 0.20 & 0.30 & -0.50 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Setting up the linear system $\pi_A + \pi_B + \pi_C = 1$:

From row 1: $-0.25 \pi_A + 0.167 \pi_B + 0.083 \pi_C = 0$
From row 2: $0.267 \pi_A - 0.40 \pi_B + 0.133 \pi_C = 0$

Solving yields the steady-state vector:
$$\pi \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.51 & 0.30 & 0.19 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Interpretation:** In the long run, Brand A retains ~51% share, B ~30%, C ~19%. Brand A is gaining dominance through strong retention ($P_{AA} = 0.75$).

---

### Step (iv): Switching Rates

Switching rate for brand $i$ = $1 - P_{ii}$.

$$SR_A = 1 - 0.75 = 0.25 \; (25\%)$$
$$SR_B = 1 - 0.60 = 0.40 \; (40\%)$$
$$SR_C = 1 - 0.50 = 0.50 \; (50\%)$$

**Analytical Insight:** Brand C has the highest switching rate (50%), making it the most vulnerable. Brand A is the most "loyalty-strong."

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Model

| Scenario | Recommended Model | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| Periodic panel data of buyer choices | **Markov Chain Model** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Repeat purchase frequency analysis | **NBD / Dirichlet Model** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Forecasting new brand adoption | **Bass Diffusion Model** | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Service switching (banking, telecom) | **Survival Analysis / Hazard Models** | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Variety seeking analysis | **Multinomial Logit Choice Model** | ⭐⭐ |

---

## 3.3 Bass Diffusion Model for Brand Switching (Brief)

For new brands entering a market, the Bass model captures switching through innovation ($p$) and imitation ($q$):

$$
\dfrac{dN(t)}{dt} = \left[ p + q \cdot \dfrac{N(t)}{m} \right] \cdot \left[ m - N(t) \right]
$$

Where:
- $m$ = market potential
- $p$ = coefficient of innovation (external influence)
- $q$ = coefficient of imitation (internal/word-of-mouth influence)

This is **highly relevant** when analyzing switching behavior caused by disruptive market entrants (e.g., Jio's entry into Indian telecom).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Brand Switching Decision Flow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Customer with Brand A] --> B{Level of Satisfaction}
    B -- High Satisfaction --> C[Continue with Brand A]
    B -- Low Satisfaction --> D{Encounter Competitor Stimulus?}
    D -- No --> C
    D -- Yes --> E[Evaluate Switching Costs]
    E -- Costs Too High --> C
    E -- Costs Acceptable --> F[Trial Purchase of Brand B]
    F --> G{Satisfied with Brand B?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Switch Permanently to Brand B]
    G -- No --> I[Return to Brand A or Try Brand C]
    C --> J[Brand A Retains Customer]
    H --> K[Brand A Loses Customer]
    I --> J
    I --> K
```

---

## 4.2 Brand Switching Type Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BS[Brand Switching] --> VOL[Voluntary Switching]
    BS --> INV[Involuntary Switching]
    BS --> VAR[Variety Seeking Switching]
    BS --> PRC[Price Driven Switching]
    BS --> SVC[Service Driven Switching]
    VOL --> VOL1[Personal Choice Driven]
    VOL --> VOL2[Recommendation Based]
    INV --> INV1[Forced Migration]
    INV --> INV2[Regulatory Change]
    INV --> INV3[Out of Stock Situations]
    VAR --> VAR1[Novelty Seeker]
    VAR --> VAR2[Sensation Seeker]
    PRC --> PRC1[Price Sensitivity]
    PRC --> PRC2[Discount Triggered]
    SVC --> SVC1[Service Failure]
    SVC --> SVC2[Complaint Unresolved]
```

---

## 4.3 Markov Transition Cycle (Mermaid State Diagram)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> BrandA
    BrandA --> BrandB : P_AB
    BrandA --> BrandC : P_AC
    BrandA --> BrandA : P_AA
    BrandB --> BrandA : P_BA
    BrandB --> BrandC : P_BC
    BrandB --> BrandB : P_BB
    BrandC --> BrandA : P_CA
    BrandC --> BrandB : P_CB
    BrandC --> BrandC : P_CC
    BrandA --> [*] : Defection
    BrandB --> [*] : Defection
    BrandC --> [*] : Defection
```

---

## 4.4 Strategy Framework for Reducing Brand Switching (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Reduce Brand Switching] --> R1[Increase Switching Costs]
    R --> R2[Enhance Loyalty Programs]
    R --> R3[Improve Customer Experience]
    R --> R4[Continuous Innovation]
    R --> R5[Emotional Brand Building]
    R1 --> R1a[Loyalty Tiers]
    R1 --> R1b[Contract Lock-ins]
    R2 --> R2a[Reward Points]
    R2 --> R2b[Member Exclusive Benefits]
    R3 --> R3a[Service Recovery]
    R3 --> R3b[Personalized Communication]
    R4 --> R4a[Product Updates]
    R4 --> R4b[Feature Additions]
    R5 --> R5a[Brand Storytelling]
    R5 --> R5b[Community Building]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define brand switching. List any four reasons why consumers switch brands.**

**Model Answer:**
Brand switching is the consumer behavior where a buyer shifts from a regularly used brand to an alternative brand within the same product category. Four reasons are:
1. **Dissatisfaction** with current brand's quality or performance.
2. **Price differential** – cheaper alternatives available.
3. **Attractive competitor offers** – better value proposition.
4. **Variety seeking** – desire for novelty and change.
5. **Poor service experience** – unresolved complaints.
6. **Influence of peer recommendations.**

*(Any four well-explained reasons — 3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for reasons)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between voluntary and involuntary brand switching with an example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Voluntary Switching | Involuntary Switching |
|---|---|---|
| **Driver** | Internal consumer choice | External compulsion |
| **Control** | Consumer has full agency | Consumer has no choice |
| **Example** | Moving from Nike to Adidas for design preference | Forced shift after telecom operator's license cancellation |
| **Brand Response** | Marketing/loyalty programs | Limited (often accepts) |

*(3 marks: 1 each for distinction + 1 for examples)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> KTU ESE format requires answering either **Question A** OR **Question B** (Module Internal Choice). Each has sub-parts of 7 marks.

---

### **Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Markov Chain Model** of brand switching in detail. How is the transition probability matrix constructed? Illustrate with a suitable example.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A market has three brands A, B, C. Given the transition matrix below, determine:
- (i) Period 2 shares if current shares are 0.4, 0.4, 0.2.
- (ii) Steady-state shares.
- (iii) Switching rates for each brand.

$$
P = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.3 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.5 & 0.3 \\ 0.1 & 0.2 & 0.7 \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

#### 📖 MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

**(a) Markov Chain Model Explanation:**

The Markov Chain Model assumes that the probability of a consumer choosing a particular brand at time $t+1$ depends only on the brand chosen at time $t$ (Markov property / memorylessness). The model uses a **Transition Probability Matrix (TPM)** $P$ where element $P_{ij}$ represents the probability of a buyer moving from brand $i$ to brand $j$.

**Construction Steps:**
1. Collect panel data tracking individual buyers across multiple purchase periods.
2. Tally the number of buyers moving from each brand $i$ to each brand $j$ ($n_{ij}$).
3. Compute $P_{ij} = \dfrac{n_{ij}}{\sum_j n_{ij}}$ (row normalization).
4. Verify $\sum_j P_{ij} = 1$ for each row.

**Market share evolution:** $S_{t+1} = S_t \cdot P$

**Steady-state:** $\pi P = \pi$, solved as an eigenvector problem.

**Example:** If $P = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 & 0.2 & 0.1 \\ 0.3 & 0.6 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.2 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}$ and current share is $(0.5, 0.3, 0.2)$, then next-period share is $S_2 = (0.51, 0.28, 0.21)$.

*(7 marks distributed: 2 for concept, 2 for TPM construction, 2 for evolution logic, 1 for example)*

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Period 2 shares:**
Given $S_1 = (0.4, 0.4, 0.2)$.

$$
S_{2A} = (0.4)(0.6) + (0.4)(0.2) + (0.2)(0.1) = 0.24 + 0.08 + 0.02 = 0.34
$$

$$
S_{2B} = (0.4)(0.3) + (0.4)(0.5) + (0.2)(0.2) = 0.12 + 0.20 + 0.04 = 0.36
$$

$$
S_{2C} = (0.4)(0.1) + (0.4)(0.3) + (0.2)(0.7) = 0.04 + 0.12 + 0.14 = 0.30
$$

So $S_2 = (0.34, 0.36, 0.30)$.

**(ii) Steady-state shares:**

Solving $\pi P = \pi$ with $\pi_A + \pi_B + \pi_C = 1$:

From Row A: $0.6 \pi_A + 0.2 \pi_B + 0.1 \pi_C = \pi_A \Rightarrow -0.4 \pi_A + 0.2 \pi_B + 0.1 \pi_C = 0$
From Row B: $0.3 \pi_A + 0.5 \pi_B + 0.2 \pi_C = \pi_B \Rightarrow 0.3 \pi_A - 0.5 \pi_B + 0.2 \pi_C = 0$

Substituting $\pi_C = 1 - \pi_A - \pi_B$:
$$-0.4\pi_A + 0.2\pi_B + 0.1(1 - \pi_A - \pi_B) = 0 \Rightarrow -0.5\pi_A + 0.1\pi_B = -0.1$$
$$0.3\pi_A - 0.5\pi_B + 0.2(1 - \pi_A - \pi_B) = 0 \Rightarrow 0.1\pi_A - 0.7\pi_B = -0.2$$

From the first: $\pi_B = 5\pi_A - 1$. Substitute into second:
$$0.1\pi_A - 0.7(5\pi_A - 1) = -0.2$$
$$0.1\pi_A - 3.5\pi_A + 0.7 = -0.2$$
$$-3.4\pi_A = -0.9$$
$$\pi_A \approx 0.265, \quad \pi_B \approx 0.323, \quad \pi_C \approx 0.412$$

**Steady-state shares ≈ (26.5%, 32.3%, 41.2%)** — Brand C dominates in the long run due to high retention ($P_{CC} = 0.7$).

**(iii) Switching rates:**
$$SR_A = 1 - 0.6 = 0.40 \;(40\%)$$
$$SR_B = 1 - 0.5 = 0.50 \;(50\%)$$
$$SR_C = 1 - 0.7 = 0.30 \;(30\%)$$

Brand C has the lowest switching rate → highest loyalty. Brand B is most vulnerable.

*(7 marks distributed: 2 for shares calc, 3 for steady-state, 2 for switching rates)*

---

### **Question B [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **factors influencing brand switching behavior** in detail. How can marketers strategically reduce switching rates?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A consumer panel of 500 households reveals the following switching data in one quarter:
- 200 out of 250 Brand X users stayed with X.
- 30 out of 250 Brand X users switched to Y.
- 20 out of 250 Brand X users switched to Z.
- Of 150 Y-users, 100 stayed, 30 went to X, 20 went to Z.
- Of 100 Z-users, 50 stayed, 25 went to X, 25 went to Y.

**Compute:**
- (i) The transition probability matrix.
- (ii) Loyalty index for each brand.
- (iii) Brand with highest switching cost perception.

---

#### 📖 MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

**(a) Factors Influencing Brand Switching:**

Brand switching behavior is influenced by a tripartite set of forces:

**1. Consumer-Related Factors:**
- **Variety-seeking tendency** – Inherent personality trait.
- **Innovativeness** – Early adopters switch more frequently.
- **Risk tolerance** – Higher tolerance = higher switching.
- **Involvement level** – Low involvement categories (salt, soap) show more switching.

**2. Brand-Related Factors:**
- **Perceived quality decline**
- **Brand fatigue**
- **Pricing strategy inconsistencies**
- **Service quality erosion**
- **Loss of differentiation**

**3. Market/External Factors:**
- **Competitive intensity**
- **Economic conditions** (recession increases price switching)
- **Social influence / word of mouth**
- **Technological disruption**
- **Cultural shifts**

**Marketing Strategies to Reduce Switching:**
1. **Loyalty Programs** – Reward points, tiered memberships.
2. **Switching Cost Inflation** – Multi-year contracts, bundled offerings.
3. **Service Recovery** – Quick grievance redressal to recover defection.
4. **Continuous Innovation** – Keep brand refreshed.
5. **Emotional Branding** – Build love marks (Kerala examples: Nilgiris, Joyalukkas).
6. **Personalization** – Data-driven customized offers.

*(7 marks: 3 for factors, 2 for framework, 2 for strategies)*

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Transition Probability Matrix:**

Total transitions from X = 250: stayed (200), to Y (30), to Z (20).
$$P_{XX} = 200/250 = 0.80, \quad P_{XY} = 30/250 = 0.12, \quad P_{XZ} = 20/250 = 0.08$$

Total transitions from Y = 150:
$$P_{YX} = 30/150 = 0.20, \quad P_{YY} = 100/150 \approx 0.667, \quad P_{YZ} = 20/150 \approx 0.133$$

Total transitions from Z = 100:
$$P_{ZX} = 25/100 = 0.25, \quad P_{ZY} = 25/100 = 0.25, \quad P_{ZZ} = 50/100 = 0.50$$

$$
P = \begin{bmatrix} 0.80 & 0.12 & 0.08 \\ 0.20 & 0.667 & 0.133 \\ 0.25 & 0.25 & 0.50 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**(ii) Loyalty Index:** $LI_i = P_{ii}$ (diagonal elements)

$$LI_X = 0.80, \quad LI_Y = 0.667, \quad LI_Z = 0.50$$

**(iii) Highest Switching Cost Perception:**

Switching Cost is **inversely related** to switching rate ($1 - LI$). Higher loyalty index = higher perceived switching cost.

Brand **X** has the highest $LI$ (0.80) → **highest switching cost perception** (likely due to emotional bond, brand trust, or structural lock-in).

*(7 marks: 2 for TPM, 3 for loyalty indices, 2 for switching cost interpretation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callouts**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students forget to verify row sums = 1 in TPM. This leads to a 1-mark deduction per row error.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing *loyalty index* with *retention rate*. Loyalty index is **behavioral** (actual repurchase); retention rate is **managerial** (a planned metric).
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** In steady-state problems, students forget the constraint $\sum \pi_j = 1$ and report non-normalized vectors — instant 2-mark loss.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Writing "brand switching" without distinguishing it from **brand switching intent**. Intent is *cognitive*; switching is *behavioral*. Examiners mark strictly.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Failing to mention **memoryless property** when explaining Markov model — a 1-mark conceptual deduction.

---

## ✅ TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] **Definition:** Brand switching = consumer migrates from habitual brand to alternative.
- [x] **Three Pillars:** Reasons, Types, Barriers.
- [x] **Five Types:** Voluntary, Involuntary, Variety-Seeking, Price-Driven, Service-Driven.
- [x] **Switching Rate Formula:** $SR = 1 - P_{ii}$ (diagonal complement).
- [x] **Retention Rate:** $RR = 1 - SR$.
- [x] **Loyalty Index:** $LI = P_{ii}$.
- [x] **Markov TPM Construction:** $P_{ij} = n_{ij} / \sum_j n_{ij}$.
- [x] **Row Sum Property:** $\sum_j P_{ij} = 1$.
- [x] **Market Share Evolution:** $S_{t+1} = S_t \cdot P$.
- [x] **Steady-State:** $\pi P = \pi$, with $\sum \pi_j = 1$.
- [x] **Bass Model:** Innovation ($p$) + Imitation ($q$) parameters for new brand switching.
- [x] **Switching Barriers:** Economic, Psychological, Effort-based, Loyalty.
- [x] **Push, Pull, Pry:** Three forces of brand switching — remember this trio.
- [x] **Real-world anchor:** Jio disrupted Indian telecom switching dynamics in 2016.

> 🎯 **Final Tip:** In KTU exams, **always state assumptions explicitly** before any Markov chain calculation. Examiners reward methodological discipline as much as numerical accuracy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measuring and Interpreting Brand Performance: Developing a Brand Equity Measurement and Management System
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET753 - PRODUCT AND BRAND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Measuring and Interpreting Brand Performance: Developing a Brand Equity Measurement and Management System

# 📊 Measuring & Interpreting Brand Performance: The Brand Equity Measurement & Management System (BEMS)

---

## 🎯 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Keller's Framework):**
> A **Brand Equity Measurement and Management System (BEMS)** is a set of organized procedures designed to provide marketers with timely, accurate, and actionable information about brands to make better strategic and tactical decisions. It is a structured marketing intelligence system that quantifies, tracks, and diagnoses the health of a brand over time.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — "The Brand as a Patient"

Imagine a **brand as a living patient** visiting a doctor:
- The **doctor** = Marketing Manager
- The **medical report** = Brand Equity Measurement System
- The **blood test/ECG** = Brand Audit (the "physical exam")
- The **regular check-up schedule** = Brand Tracking (continuous monitoring)
- The **prescribed lifestyle changes** = Brand Equity Management Actions

Just as a doctor cannot treat a patient without periodic tests, no brand manager can manage brand equity without a **structured measurement and feedback loop**. BEMS is essentially the **medical record-keeping system for a brand**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is BEMS critical for KTU exams?**
> Because KTU examiners (as per 2024 Scheme CO3: *Develop Brand Strategy*) test whether students can move from theory (brand equity concepts) to **application** (how do you actually measure a brand like *Nescafe*, *Apple*, or *Nirma*?).

### 🔑 The Two-Pillar Architecture of BEMS

According to Keller (2013) and Aaker (1996), BEMS rests on **two mutually reinforcing pillars**:

| Pillar | Purpose | Frequency | Nature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Brand Audit** | Diagnostic — "How is the brand doing *right now*?" | Periodic (Quarterly/Annual) | Snapshot/Static |
| **Brand Tracking** | Monitoring — "How is the brand doing *over time*?" | Continuous (Monthly/Weekly) | Longitudinal/Dynamic |

---

## 🔍 The Brand Audit — The "Health Check-up"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Brand Audit** is a focused, comprehensive, and systematic examination of a brand's current marketing program and performance to assess its health, identify its sources of brand equity, and suggest short-term and long-term corrective actions.

**The Brand Audit is conducted in TWO sequential steps:**

### Step 1: The Brand Inventory (Internal Physical Exam)

A **profile of how the brand is being marketed currently** — i.e., a comprehensive review of all marketing activities touching the consumer.

**Components of the Inventory include:**
1. **Product Audit** — Review of product performance, features, quality, design, packaging
2. **Price Audit** — Comparative pricing analysis vs. competitors
3. **Distribution Audit** — Channel coverage and intensity
4. **Communications Audit** — Review of all promotional activities (Advertising, PR, Digital, Sales Promotion)

### Step 2: The Brand Exploratory (Consumer Perspective)

A **detailed study of how customers view the brand** — uncovering brand associations, perceptions, strengths, and weaknesses from the consumer's mind.

**Three Components of the Exploratory:**

| Component | Key Question | Technique |
|---|---|---|
| **Brand Identity** | What does the brand stand for? | Internal stakeholder interviews |
| **Brand Meaning** | What does it mean to consumers? | Laddering, focus groups |
| **Brand Positioning** | How is it differentiated? | Positioning maps, perceptual maps |

### 📋 The Brand Audit — Output Summary

The audit culminates in a **Brand Audit Report** containing:
- Core brand equity associations (strength, favorability, uniqueness)
- Brand's current positioning
- Gaps between intended and actual positioning
- Recommendations for short-term tactical fixes and long-term strategic shifts

---

## 📈 The Brand Tracking — The "Continuous Health Monitor"

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Brand Tracking** is the continuous, systematic collection of consumer brand information (attitudes, awareness, usage, loyalty) over time to provide consistent baseline information about how brands and marketing programs are performing.

Unlike the audit, tracking is **quantitative, ongoing, and comparable**.

### The Four Pillars of Brand Tracking (KTU High-Yield)

| Tracking Dimension | What it Measures | Typical Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Brand Awareness** | Recognition & Recall | Aided/Unaided Recall % |
| **2. Brand Usage** | Penetration & Frequency | TRI*M (Trial, Regular, Most Used) |
| **3. Brand Attitude/Image** | Perception & Associations | Attribute ratings (Likert scale) |
| **4. Brand Loyalty/Equity** | Switching behaviour, advocacy | NPS (Net Promoter Score), Repurchase % |

---

## 🎛 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The Strategic Logic — Why Two Stages?

```
WHY TWO-STAGE BEMS?
===================

Stage 1: BRAND AUDIT   → Identifies the PROBLEM  (Diagnostic)
Stage 2: BRAND TRACKING → Monitors the SOLUTION  (Continuous Feedback)

Logic: Audit (Diagnose) → Tracking (Monitor) → Manage (Act)
       |______ MEASURE ________|     |______ MANAGE ______|
```

### 📊 The Brand Equity Measurement System — Process Architecture

A BEMS follows a **four-stage cyclical pipeline**:

#### Stage 1: Conduct Brand Audits (Quarterly)
- Internal marketing program review
- External consumer perception review
- **Output:** Brand audit report identifying strengths, weaknesses, gaps

#### Stage 2: Perform Brand Tracking (Continuous)
- Quantitative consumer surveys across key dimensions
- **Output:** Brand equity tracking dashboard (charts of equity drivers over time)

#### Stage 3: Establish Brand Equity Management Decisions
- Translate audit + tracking data into managerial decisions
- **Output:** Strategic brand action plan

#### Stage 4: Capture Customer Mind-Set Metrics
- Re-measure customer-based brand equity (CBBE) using the Brand Resonance Model
- **Output:** CBBE pyramid status report

---

### 📐 KTU Formula Sheet — Key Brand Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Purpose | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Top-of-Mind Awareness (TOMA)** | $\text{TOMA} = \frac{\text{Customers naming brand first}}{\text{Total customers surveyed}} \times 100$ | Measures salience | $\text{Range} = 0\% \text{ to } 100\%$ |
| **Aided Brand Recall (ABR)** | $\text{ABR} = \frac{\text{Customers recognizing brand from list}}{\text{Total respondents}} \times 100$ | Recognition measure | Excludes those with zero prior exposure |
| **Unaided Brand Recall (UBR)** | $\text{UBR} = \frac{\text{Customers recalling brand unprompted}}{\text{Total respondents}} \times 100$ | Memory strength | Stricter measure than aided recall |
| **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** | $\text{NPS} = \text{Percent Promoters} - \text{Percent Detractors}$ | Loyalty proxy | $\text{Range} = -100 \text{ to } +100$ |
| **Brand Penetration Rate** | $\text{Penetration} = \frac{\text{Customers who tried the brand}}{\text{Total target population}} \times 100$ | Adoption measure | Lower bound = 0% |
| **Repeat Purchase Rate (RPR)** | $\text{RPR} = \frac{\text{Customers buying} \geq 2 \text{ times}}{\text{Total customers}} \times 100$ | Loyalty intensity | Excludes one-time buyers |
| **Brand Equity (Aaker's 5-Asset Model)** | $\text{BE} = f(L_A, P_Q, B_A, B_L, O_M)^*$ | Composite valuation | Multidimensional construct |
| **TRI*M Index** | $\text{TRI*M} = \text{Trial\%} + \text{Regular\%} + \text{Top-of-Mind\%} - \text{Defection\%}$ | Brand health composite | Optimal TRI*M $> 100$ |

> *$L_A$ = Loyalty, $P_Q$ = Perceived Quality, $B_A$ = Brand Awareness, $B_L$ = Brand Associations, $O_M$ = Other Proprietary Assets

---

### The Brand Asset Valuator (BAV) — Conceptual Framework

> [!NOTE]
> The **BAV model** by Young & Rubicam (Y&R) classifies brands into **four quadrants** based on two axes.

| Quadrant | Strength (X-axis) | Stature (Y-axis) | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Leader** | High | High | Strong, established brand (e.g., Google, Apple) |
| **Aspirational** | Low | High | High stature but perceived as weak (e.g., Tesla early days) |
| **Niche** | Low | Low | Niche players with limited share |
| **Eroded** | High | Low | Was strong, now declining (e.g., Kodak) |

### The Brand Resonance Model (CBBE Pyramid) — Keller

```
              ┌──────────────────┐
              │  RESONANCE       │  ← Top: Loyalty & Advocacy
              ├──────────────────┤
              │  JUDGMENTS       │  ← Feelings
              │  FEELINGS        │
              ├──────────────────┤
              │  PERFORMANCE     │  ← Brand Imagery
              │  IMAGERY         │
              ├──────────────────┤
              │  IDENTITY        │  ← Brand Salience
              │  SALIENCE        │
              └──────────────────┘
                  (Foundation)
```

### The Brand Value Chain (BVCC) — Keller's Strategic Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Brand Value Chain (BVCC) is a **causal chain** that traces how marketing investments translate into shareholder value. It has **four stages**:

$$\text{Marketing Program Investment} \rightarrow \text{Customer Mindset} \rightarrow \text{Market Performance} \rightarrow \text{Shareholder Value}$$

| Stage | Inputs | Outputs |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Marketing Investment** | R&D, Advertising, Promotions | Programme Quality & Synergy |
| **2. Customer Mindset** | Awareness, Associations, Loyalty | Customer Brand Equity |
| **3. Market Performance** | Price premium, share, growth | Market-based Brand Equity |
| **4. Shareholder Value** | Stock price, cash flow | Shareholder Value |

---

## 🧮 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation A: Calculating Net Promoter Score (NPS) — Full Worked Example

**Scenario (KTU-Style Problem):**
A FMCG company surveyed **1000 customers** about a new shampoo brand. Responses:

- **Promoters (score 9–10):** 450 customers
- **Passives (score 7–8):** 350 customers
- **Detractors (score 0–6):** 200 customers

**Calculate the NPS.**

---

#### Step 1: Identify the Formula

From the KTU Formula Sheet:

$$\text{NPS} = \text{\% Promoters} - \text{\% Detractors}$$

*Note: Passives (7–8) are EXCLUDED from the calculation.*

---

#### Step 2: Calculate % Promoters

$$\text{\% Promoters} = \frac{\text{Number of Promoters}}{\text{Total Respondents}} \times 100$$

$$\text{\% Promoters} = \frac{450}{1000} \times 100 = 45\%$$

---

#### Step 3: Calculate % Detractors

$$\text{\% Detractors} = \frac{\text{Number of Detractors}}{\text{Total Respondents}} \times 100$$

$$\text{\% Detractors} = \frac{200}{1000} \times 100 = 20\%$$

---

#### Step 4: Compute NPS

$$\text{NPS} = 45\% - 20\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{NPS} = +25}$$

---

#### Step 5: Interpret the Result

> [!NOTE]
> **NPS Interpretation Scale:**
> - Below **0**: Poor — more detractors than promoters
> - **0 to 30**: Good (typical FMCG range)
> - **30 to 50**: Great (Apple, Tesla territory)
> - **Above 50**: World-class (rare)
>
> **Our score of +25** indicates a **healthy brand** but room for improvement by converting passives (350) into promoters.

---

### Derivation B: TRI*M Index Calculation — Full Worked Example

**Scenario:**
A beverage brand tracks 1000 respondents in three categories:

- **Trial (ever tried the brand):** 700
- **Regular usage (drink at least once a week):** 400
- **Top-of-Mind (named first):** 200
- **Defection (switched to competitor):** 100

---

#### Step 1: Convert Counts to Percentages

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Trial \%} &= \frac{700}{1000} \times 100 = 70\% \\
\text{Regular \%} &= \frac{400}{1000} \times 100 = 40\% \\
\text{TOM \%} &= \frac{200}{1000} \times 100 = 20\% \\
\text{Defection \%} &= \frac{100}{1000} \times 100 = 10\%
\end{aligned}$$

---

#### Step 2: Apply TRI*M Formula

$$\text{TRI*M} = \text{Trial\%} + \text{Regular\%} + \text{TOM\%} - \text{Defection\%}$$

$$\text{TRI*M} = 70 + 40 + 20 - 10$$

$$\boxed{\text{TRI*M} = 120}$$

---

#### Step 3: Interpret

TRI*M > 100 is considered **excellent brand health**. Our beverage brand demonstrates strong performance with a TRI*M index of **120**.

---

### Derivation C: Brand Equity Tenancy — Aaker's Composite Model

**Aaker's Brand Equity Tenancy Equation:**

$$\text{Brand Equity (BE)} = \text{Loyalty} + \text{Perceived Quality} + \text{Brand Awareness} + \text{Brand Associations} + \text{Other Assets (patents, trademarks)}$$

This is a **composite**, not a numerical sum — each dimension is measured on its own scale:

$$\text{BE} = f(L, Q, A, A_s, M)$$

Where:
- $L$ = Loyalty (measured via NPS, repeat purchase, switch resistance)
- $Q$ = Perceived Quality (measured via attribute ratings)
- $A$ = Awareness (measured via recall scores)
- $A_s$ = Brand Associations (measured via image profiling)
- $M$ = Market-based Assets (measured via price premium, distribution)

---

### 📊 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Metric

| Business Question | Best Metric | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Is our brand remembered? | Brand Recall (Aided/Unaided) | Captures memory strength |
| Is our brand profitable? | Price Premium | Direct financial measure |
| Will customers switch? | NPS | Forward-looking loyalty |
| Is market share growing? | Penetration Rate | Adoption measure |
| Are customers loyal? | Repeat Purchase Rate | Behavioral loyalty |
| Is our brand distinctive? | Perceptual Map | Position differentiation |
| How does brand affect stock price? | Brand Value Chain (Stage 4) | Shareholder value |

---

## 📐 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: BEMS — Master Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Strategic Marketing Objectives] --> B[Brand Equity Measurement and Management System]
    
    B --> C[Stage 1: Brand Audit]
    C --> C1[Brand Inventory]
    C --> C2[Brand Exploratory]
    C1 --> C3[Audit Report]
    C2 --> C3
    
    C3 --> D[Stage 2: Brand Tracking]
    D --> D1[Awareness Metrics]
    D --> D2[Usage Metrics]
    D --> D3[Attitude Metrics]
    D --> D4[Loyalty Metrics]
    
    D1 --> E[Brand Equity Management Decisions]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    
    E --> F[Tactical Brand Decisions]
    E --> G[Strategic Brand Decisions]
    E --> H[Financial Brand Valuation]
```

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**"Distinguish between Brand Audit and Brand Tracking."**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Brand Audit | Brand Tracking |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Diagnostic — identifies current brand health | Monitoring — tracks brand over time |
| **Frequency** | Periodic (quarterly/annually) | Continuous (monthly/weekly) |
| **Nature** | Mostly qualitative | Mostly quantitative |
| **Perspective** | Internal + External | Primarily consumer |
| **Output** | Brand Audit Report | Brand Equity Dashboard |

**(Any 3 valid differences = 3 marks)**

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]**
**"What is Brand Equity Measurement and Management System (BEMS)?"**

**Model Answer:**

A Brand Equity Measurement and Management System (BEMS) is a structured set of marketing research procedures that provides timely, accurate, and actionable information about brands for better strategic decision-making. It consists of two pillars — **Brand Audit** (diagnostic, periodic) and **Brand Tracking** (continuous, longitudinal) — which together feed the **Brand Equity Management System** to make tactical and strategic decisions.

---

### 📕 PART B — Essay-Type Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulations**, Part B questions carry 14 marks and may offer an internal choice. Below are **Question A** and **Question B** — both full-length, board-exam quality.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Paper]*

#### **(a) Explain the concept of Brand Equity Measurement and Management System (BEMS). Discuss in detail the components of the Brand Audit process. (7 marks)**

#### **(b) Describe the Brand Tracking system. Explain any four key metrics used in brand tracking with examples. (7 marks)**

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION (A):**

**Part (a) — Brand Audit (7 Marks)**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The Brand Equity Measurement and Management System (BEMS) is a structured intelligence system that quantifies, monitors, and manages brand health. The Brand Audit is its diagnostic pillar — a comprehensive examination of a brand's marketing program and consumer perception.

**Brand Inventory (3 marks):**
The Brand Inventory is a **profile of current marketing activities**, comprising four sub-audits:
1. **Product Audit** — evaluates features, design, quality, packaging
2. **Price Audit** — analyzes relative pricing vs. competitors
3. **Distribution Audit** — assesses channel coverage and intensity
4. **Communications Audit** — reviews advertising, sales promotion, PR, digital marketing

**Brand Exploratory (3 marks):**
The Brand Exploratory captures the **consumer's perspective** through:
1. **Brand Identity** — what the brand stands for (interviews with internal stakeholders)
2. **Brand Meaning** — what the brand means to consumers (focus groups, laddering)
3. **Brand Positioning** — how it is differentiated in the market (perceptual maps)

**Conclusion (Bonus):** The Brand Audit report identifies gaps and proposes corrective actions.

---

**Part (b) — Brand Tracking & Metrics (7 Marks)**

**Concept (1 mark):**
Brand Tracking is the continuous, systematic collection of consumer brand information over time to provide consistent baseline data on brand performance.

**Four Key Metrics (4 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Brand Awareness** — Measured via Aided Recall, Unaided Recall, Top-of-Mind Awareness (TOMA). Example: A FMCG brand's TOMA rose from 25% to 38% after a celebrity campaign.

2. **Brand Usage** — Measured via TRI*M index (Trial, Regular, Most Used). Example: A beverage brand's TRI*M = 120 indicates healthy adoption.

3. **Brand Attitude/Image** — Measured via Likert-scale ratings on attributes like quality, value, innovation.

4. **Brand Loyalty** — Measured via Net Promoter Score (NPS). Example: NPS of +50 is world-class (Apple range).

**Why Tracking Matters (2 marks):**
Tracking provides longitudinal data to detect trends early — e.g., declining awareness before share loss becomes visible.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]*

#### **(a) Explain Keller's Brand Value Chain model with its four stages. How does it help in measuring brand performance? (7 marks)**

#### **(b) Discuss the Customer-Based Brand Equity (CBBE) Pyramid model. Explain the four levels of the pyramid with examples. (7 marks)**

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION (B):**

**Part (a) — Brand Value Chain (7 Marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):**
The Brand Value Chain (BVCC) is a strategic framework by Keller that traces how **marketing investments translate into shareholder value** through a four-stage causal chain.

**Four Stages (4 marks — 1 each):**

**Stage 1 — Marketing Investment:** Inputs include R&D expenditure, advertising spend, promotional budgets. Outputs are Program Quality and Brand Investment levels.

**Stage 2 — Customer Mindset:** Outputs include Awareness, Brand Associations, Perceived Quality, and Loyalty. This stage is the **heart of CBBE**.

**Stage 3 — Market Performance:** Outputs include Price Premium, Market Share, Growth Rate, and Expansion Success. This is where brand equity becomes financial performance.

**Stage 4 — Shareholder Value:** Outputs include Stock Price, Cash Flow, and overall Brand Valuation. This is the ultimate outcome.

**Application (2 marks):**
BVCC helps managers **diagnose where brand value is leaking** — e.g., high advertising spend (Stage 1) but weak loyalty (Stage 2) suggests poor creative quality. This diagnostic power makes BVCC a key BEMS tool.

---

**Part (b) — CBBE Pyramid (7 Marks)**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Keller's CBBE Pyramid conceptualizes brand equity as a **four-level cognitive structure** that customers build in their minds.

**Four Levels (4 marks — 1 each):**

**Level 1 — Brand Salience (Foundation):**
Refers to brand awareness and recognition. *Example:* When you say "smartphone," iPhone and Samsung come to mind immediately.

**Level 2 — Brand Meaning (Performance + Imagery):**
- **Performance:** Product-level attributes — reliability, durability, features. *Example:* Toyota's "reliability."
- **Imagery:** Higher-level abstract associations — lifestyle, personality. *Example:* Nike = athletic heroism.

**Level 3 — Brand Response (Judgments + Feelings):**
- **Judgments:** Rational evaluations — quality, credibility, superiority.
- **Feelings:** Emotional responses — excitement, warmth, security. *Example:* Coca-Cola evokes happiness.

**Level 4 — Brand Resonance (Top):**
The pinnacle — deep psychological bond leading to **loyalty and advocacy**. *Example:* Apple fans queuing for new iPhone launches.

**Strategic Implication (2 marks):**
A BEMS should measure **all four levels** — without salience, no meaning can be built; without meaning, no response; without response, no resonance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing Audit with Tracking** — The single most common mistake. Remember: Audit = Diagnostic, Tracking = Monitoring.
> 2. **Skipping CBBE Levels** — Do NOT discuss Brand Resonance without first establishing Salience, Meaning, and Response.
> 3. **Forgetting the Feedback Loop** — BEMS is **cyclical**, not linear. The audit findings feed tracking, which informs management decisions, which loop back to re-audit.
> 4. **Wrong NPS Interpretation** — NPS ranges from -100 to +100. Saying "NPS is 75%" will cost you 2 marks.
> 5. **Generic Real-World Examples** — Always use **brand-specific** examples (Apple, Nike, Coca-Cola) — KTU examiners reward applied thinking.

---

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- ✅ **BEMS = Brand Audit + Brand Tracking + Management Decisions**
- ✅ **Brand Audit** = Diagnostic, Periodic (Quarterly/Annual), Qualitative-leaning
- ✅ **Brand Tracking** = Continuous, Quantitative, Longitudinal
- ✅ **Brand Audit has two steps:** Inventory (Marketing Program) + Exploratory (Consumer View)
- ✅ **Brand Inventory covers 4Ps:** Product, Price, Place, Promotion
- ✅ **Brand Exploratory has 3 components:** Identity, Meaning, Positioning
- ✅ **Brand Tracking has 4 pillars:** Awareness, Usage, Attitude, Loyalty

- ✅ **BAV Model:** 4 Quadrants (Leader, Aspirational, Niche, Eroded) on Strength × Stature axes

- ✅ **BVCC (Keller):** Marketing Investment → Customer Mindset → Market Performance → Shareholder Value

## 🎯 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Keller's Framework):**
> A **Brand Equity Measurement and Management System (BEMS)** is a set of organized procedures designed to provide marketers with timely, accurate, and actionable information about brands to make better strategic and tactical decisions. It is a structured marketing intelligence system that quantifies, tracks, and diagnoses the health of a brand over time.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — "The Brand as a Patient"

Imagine a **brand as a living patient** visiting a doctor:
- The **doctor** = Marketing Manager
- The **medical report** = Brand Equity Measurement System
- The **blood test/ECG** = Brand Audit (the "physical exam")
- The **regular check-up schedule** = Brand Tracking (continuous monitoring)
- The **prescribed lifestyle changes** = Brand Equity Management Actions

Just as a doctor cannot treat a patient without periodic tests, no brand manager can manage brand equity without a **structured measurement and feedback loop**. BEMS is essentially the **medical record-keeping system for a brand**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is BEMS critical for KTU exams?**
> Because KTU examiners (as per 2024 Scheme CO3: *Develop Brand Strategy*) test whether students can move from theory (brand equity concepts) to **application** (how do you actually measure a brand like *Nescafe*, *Apple*, or *Nirma*?).

### 🔑 The Two-Pillar Architecture of BEMS

According to Keller (2013) and Aaker (1996), BEMS rests on **two mutually reinforcing pillars**:

| Pillar | Purpose | Frequency | Nature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Brand Audit** | Diagnostic — "How is the brand doing *right now*?" | Periodic (Quarterly/Annual) | Snapshot/Static |
| **Brand Tracking** | Monitoring — "How is the brand doing *over time*?" | Continuous (Monthly/Weekly) | Longitudinal/Dynamic |

---

## 🔍 The Brand Audit — The "Health Check-up"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Brand Audit** is a focused, comprehensive, and systematic examination of a brand's current marketing program and performance to assess its health, identify its sources of brand equity, and suggest short-term and long-term corrective actions.

**The Brand Audit is conducted in TWO sequential steps:**

### Step 1: The Brand Inventory (Internal Physical Exam)

A **profile of how the brand is being marketed currently** — i.e., a comprehensive review of all marketing activities touching the consumer.

**Components of the Inventory include:**
1. **Product Audit** — Review of product performance, features, quality, design, packaging
2. **Price Audit** — Comparative pricing analysis vs. competitors
3. **Distribution Audit** — Channel coverage and intensity
4. **Communications Audit** — Review of all promotional activities (Advertising, PR, Digital, Sales Promotion)

### Step 2: The Brand Exploratory (Consumer Perspective)

A **detailed study of how customers view the brand** — uncovering brand associations, perceptions, strengths, and weaknesses from the consumer's mind.

**Three Components of the Exploratory:**

| Component | Key Question | Technique |
|---|---|---|
| **Brand Identity** | What does the brand stand for? | Internal stakeholder interviews |
| **Brand Meaning** | What does it mean to consumers? | Laddering, focus groups |
| **Brand Positioning** | How is it differentiated? | Positioning maps, perceptual maps |

### 📋 The Brand Audit — Output Summary

The audit culminates in a **Brand Audit Report** containing:
- Core brand equity associations (strength, favorability, uniqueness)
- Brand's current positioning
- Gaps between intended and actual positioning
- Recommendations for short-term tactical fixes and long-term strategic shifts

---

## 📈 The Brand Tracking — The "Continuous Health Monitor"

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Brand Tracking** is the continuous, systematic collection of consumer brand information (attitudes, awareness, usage, loyalty) over time to provide consistent baseline information about how brands and marketing programs are performing.

Unlike the audit, tracking is **quantitative, ongoing, and comparable**.

### The Four Pillars of Brand Tracking (KTU High-Yield)

| Tracking Dimension | What it Measures | Typical Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Brand Awareness** | Recognition & Recall | Aided/Unaided Recall % |
| **2. Brand Usage** | Penetration & Frequency | TRI*M (Trial, Regular, Most Used) |
| **3. Brand Attitude/Image** | Perception & Associations | Attribute ratings (Likert scale) |
| **4. Brand Loyalty/Equity** | Switching behaviour, advocacy | NPS (Net Promoter Score), Repurchase % |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 🔍 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The Strategic Logic — Why Two Stages?

```
WHY TWO-STAGE BEMS?
===================

Stage 1: BRAND AUDIT   → Identifies the PROBLEM  (Diagnostic)
Stage 2: BRAND TRACKING → Monitors the SOLUTION  (Continuous Feedback)

Logic: Audit (Diagnose) → Tracking (Monitor) → Manage (Act)
       |______ MEASURE ________|     |______ MANAGE ______|
```

### 📊 The Brand Equity Measurement System — Process Architecture

A BEMS follows a **four-stage cyclical pipeline**:

#### Stage 1: Conduct Brand Audits (Quarterly)
- Internal marketing program review
- External consumer perception review
- **Output:** Brand audit report identifying strengths, weaknesses, gaps

#### Stage 2: Perform Brand Tracking (Continuous)
- Quantitative consumer surveys across key dimensions
- **Output:** Brand equity tracking dashboard (charts of equity drivers over time)

#### Stage 3: Establish Brand Equity Management Decisions
- Translate audit + tracking data into managerial decisions
- **Output:** Strategic brand action plan

#### Stage 4: Capture Customer Mind-Set Metrics
- Re-measure customer-based brand equity (CBBE) using the Brand Resonance Model
- **Output:** CBBE pyramid status report

---

### 📐 KTU Formula Sheet — Key Brand Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Purpose | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Top-of-Mind Awareness (TOMA)** | $\text{TOMA} = \frac{\text{Customers naming brand first}}{\text{Total customers surveyed}} \times 100$ | Measures salience | $\text{Range} = 0\% \text{ to } 100\%$ |
| **Aided Brand Recall (ABR)** | $\text{ABR} = \frac{\text{Customers recognizing brand from list}}{\text{Total respondents}} \times 100$ | Recognition measure | Excludes those with zero prior exposure |
| **Unaided Brand Recall (UBR)** | $\text{UBR} = \frac{\text{Customers recalling brand unprompted}}{\text{Total respondents}} \times 100$ | Memory strength | Stricter measure than aided recall |
| **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** | $\text{NPS} = \text{Percent Promoters} - \text{Percent Detractors}$ | Loyalty proxy | $\text{Range} = -100 \text{ to } +100$ |
| **Brand Penetration Rate** | $\text{Penetration} = \frac{\text{Customers who tried the brand}}{\text{Total target population}} \times 100$ | Adoption measure | Lower bound = 0% |
| **Repeat Purchase Rate (RPR)** | $\text{RPR} = \frac{\text{Customers buying} \geq 2 \text{ times}}{\text{Total customers}} \times 100$ | Loyalty intensity | Excludes one-time buyers |
| **Brand Equity (Aaker's 5-Asset Model)** | $\text{BE} = f(L_A, P_Q, B_A, B_L, O_M)^*$ | Composite valuation | Multidimensional construct |
| **TRI*M Index** | $\text{TRI*M} = \text{Trial\%} + \text{Regular\%} + \text{Top-of-Mind\%} - \text{Defection\%}$ | Brand health composite | Optimal TRI*M $> 100$ |

> *$L_A$ = Loyalty, $P_Q$ = Perceived Quality, $B_A$ = Brand Awareness, $B_L$ = Brand Associations, $O_M$ = Other Proprietary Assets

---

### The Brand Asset Valuator (BAV) — Conceptual Framework

> [!NOTE]
> The **BAV model** by Young & Rubicam (Y&R) classifies brands into **four quadrants** based on two axes.

| Quadrant | Strength (X-axis) | Stature (Y-axis) | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Leader** | High | High | Strong, established brand (e.g., Google, Apple) |
| **Aspirational** | Low | High | High stature but perceived as weak (e.g., Tesla early days) |
| **Niche** | Low | Low | Niche players with limited share |
| **Eroded** | High | Low | Was strong, now declining (e.g., Kodak) |

### The Brand Resonance Model (CBBE Pyramid) — Keller

```
              ┌──────────────────┐
              │  RESONANCE       │  ← Top: Loyalty & Advocacy
              ├──────────────────┤
              │  JUDGMENTS       │  ← Feelings
              │  FEELINGS        │
              ├──────────────────┤
              │  PERFORMANCE     │  ← Brand Imagery
              │  IMAGERY         │
              ├──────────────────┤
              │  IDENTITY        │  ← Brand Salience
              │  SALIENCE        │
              └──────────────────┘
                  (Foundation)
```

### The Brand Value Chain (BVCC) — Keller's Strategic Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Brand Value Chain (BVCC) is a **causal chain** that traces how marketing investments translate into shareholder value. It has **four stages**:

$$\text{Marketing Program Investment} \rightarrow \text{Customer Mindset} \rightarrow \text{Market Performance} \rightarrow \text{Shareholder Value}$$

| Stage | Inputs | Outputs |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Marketing Investment** | R&D, Advertising, Promotions | Programme Quality & Synergy |
| **2. Customer Mindset** | Awareness, Associations, Loyalty | Customer Brand Equity |
| **3. Market Performance** | Price premium, share, growth | Market-based Brand Equity |
| **4. Shareholder Value** | Stock price, cash flow | Shareholder Value |

---

## 🧮 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation A: Calculating Net Promoter Score (NPS) — Full Worked Example

**Scenario (KTU-Style Problem):**
A FMCG company surveyed **1000 customers** about a new shampoo brand. Responses:

- **Promoters (score 9–10):** 450 customers
- **Passives (score 7–8):** 350 customers
- **Detractors (score 0–6):** 200 customers

**Calculate the NPS.**

---

#### Step 1: Identify the Formula

From the KTU Formula Sheet:

$$\text{NPS} = \text{\% Promoters} - \text{\% Detractors}$$

*Note: Passives (7–8) are EXCLUDED from the calculation.*

---

#### Step 2: Calculate % Promoters

$$\text{\% Promoters} = \frac{\text{Number of Promoters}}{\text{Total Respondents}} \times 100$$

$$\text{\% Promoters} = \frac{450}{1000} \times 100 = 45\%$$

---

#### Step 3: Calculate % Detractors

$$\text{\% Detractors} = \frac{\text{Number of Detractors}}{\text{Total Respondents}} \times 100$$

$$\text{\% Detractors} = \frac{200}{1000} \times 100 = 20\%$$

---

#### Step 4: Compute NPS

$$\text{NPS} = 45\% - 20\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{NPS} = +25}$$

---

#### Step 5: Interpret the Result

> [!NOTE]
> **NPS Interpretation Scale:**
> - Below **0**: Poor — more detractors than promoters
> - **0 to 30**: Good (typical FMCG range)
> - **30 to 50**: Great (Apple, Tesla territory)
> - **Above 50**: World-class (rare)
>
> **Our score of +25** indicates a **healthy brand** but room for improvement by converting passives (350) into promoters.

---

### Derivation B: TRI*M Index Calculation — Full Worked Example

**Scenario:**
A beverage brand tracks 1000 respondents in three categories:

- **Trial (ever tried the brand):** 700
- **Regular usage (drink at least once a week):** 400
- **Top-of-Mind (named first):** 200
- **Defection (switched to competitor):** 100

---

#### Step 1: Convert Counts to Percentages

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Trial \%} &= \frac{700}{1000} \times 100 = 70\% \\
\text{Regular \%} &= \frac{400}{1000} \times 100 = 40\% \\
\text{TOM \%} &= \frac{200}{1000} \times 100 = 20\% \\
\text{Defection \%} &= \frac{100}{1000} \times 100 = 10\%
\end{aligned}$$

---

#### Step 2: Apply TRI*M Formula

$$\text{TRI*M} = \text{Trial\%} + \text{Regular\%} + \text{TOM\%} - \text{Defection\%}$$

$$\text{TRI*M} = 70 + 40 + 20 - 10$$

$$\boxed{\text{TRI*M} = 120}$$

---

#### Step 3: Interpret

TRI*M > 100 is considered **excellent brand health**. Our beverage brand demonstrates strong performance with a TRI*M index of **120**.

---

### Derivation C: Brand Equity Tenancy — Aaker's Composite Model

**Aaker's Brand Equity Tenancy Equation:**

$$\text{Brand Equity (BE)} = \text{Loyalty} + \text{Perceived Quality} + \text{Brand Awareness} + \text{Brand Associations} + \text{Other Assets (patents, trademarks)}$$

This is a **composite**, not a numerical sum — each dimension is measured on its own scale:

$$\text{BE} = f(L, Q, A, A_s, M)$$

Where:
- $L$ = Loyalty (measured via NPS, repeat purchase, switch resistance)
- $Q$ = Perceived Quality (measured via attribute ratings)
- $A$ = Awareness (measured via recall scores)
- $A_s$ = Brand Associations (measured via image profiling)
- $M$ = Market-based Assets (measured via price premium, distribution)

---

### 📊 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Metric

| Business Question | Best Metric | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Is our brand remembered? | Brand Recall (Aided/Unaided) | Captures memory strength |
| Is our brand profitable? | Price Premium | Direct financial measure |
| Will customers switch? | NPS | Forward-looking loyalty |
| Is market share growing? | Penetration Rate | Adoption measure |
| Are customers loyal? | Repeat Purchase Rate | Behavioral loyalty |
| Is our brand distinctive? | Perceptual Map | Position differentiation |
| How does brand affect stock price? | Brand Value Chain (Stage 4) | Shareholder value |
